class_schedule.topic: Supply Chain Strategy: Efficient vs Responsive, Push vs Pull
learning_objectives: Define the supply chain and identify the flows of product, information, and funds among its stages
Distinguish between efficient and responsive supply chains and identify the drivers that must be configured for each
Explain the concepts of push, pull, and push-pull boundaries in supply chain design
Analyze the strategic fit between a firm's competitive strategy and its supply chain strategy using the implied demand uncertainty framework
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management: Strategy, Planning, and Operation (7th ed.), Ch. 1-2
Fisher, 'What Is the Right Supply Chain for Your Product?' Harvard Business Review (March-April 1997)
Simchi-Levi, Kaminsky & Simchi-Levi, Designing and Managing the Supply Chain (3rd ed.), Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: A supply chain is more than a list of suppliers. It is the entire network of organizations, people, activities, information flows, and financial flows that move a product or service from raw materials through production and distribution to the final customer. Zara, the Spanish fast-fashion retailer owned by Inditex, can design a new garment, manufacture it, and have it on store shelves in 15 days, while traditional apparel retailers take 6 to 9 months. That 10x speed advantage does not come from a single clever trick. It comes from hundreds of coordinated choices: vertically integrated dyeing and cutting in Galicia, small batch production, air freight from Spain to global stores twice a week, point-of-sale data that feeds design decisions, empowered store managers who report customer reactions daily, and postponement of color and print decisions until the last possible moment. Every one of those choices trades off cost against responsiveness, and every one reflects a deliberate strategic position. In contrast, Walmart's supply chain is engineered for the opposite extreme: lowest cost per case delivered, achieved through enormous volume commitments, cross-docking that moves product from inbound trailer to outbound trailer without touching a shelf, and a Retail Link system that shares daily POS data with 30,000 suppliers so they can manage their own inventory at Walmart's distribution centers. Both Zara and Walmart have excellent supply chains, but they would be disasters if swapped: Zara's approach would bankrupt Walmart on cost, and Walmart's approach would leave Zara months behind fashion trends. This first session frames the central question of the course: how do you design a supply chain whose capabilities actually match the competitive position your business is trying to win?
key_concepts: Supply chain definition: all parties directly or indirectly involved in fulfilling a customer request, including manufacturers, suppliers, transporters, warehouses, retailers, and customers themselves; the chain metaphor understates the network structure of real supply chains with multiple suppliers at each tier

Three flows in a supply chain: product flow (typically downstream from raw materials to customer, with returns flowing upstream), information flow (demand signals, order status, forecasts flowing both ways), and financial flow (payments upstream, credit terms, and working capital decisions)

Efficient supply chain: minimizes cost, maximizes asset utilization, uses high-volume standardized transportation, holds minimal inventory, and works best for functional products with stable predictable demand such as commodity chemicals, basic groceries, and paper products

Responsive supply chain: maximizes speed, flexibility, and service level, holds strategic buffer inventory, uses faster and more expensive transport modes, and works best for innovative products with unpredictable demand such as fashion apparel, consumer electronics at launch, and hit-driven entertainment

Implied demand uncertainty: the portion of demand uncertainty that the supply chain must absorb; it grows with the variety of products offered, the shortness of product life cycles, the required service level, and the rate of innovation

Marshall Fisher's framework: functional products need efficient supply chains and innovative products need responsive supply chains; mismatches are the single largest source of supply chain strategic error

Push system: production and distribution decisions are made based on long-term forecasts, with product moved through the chain in anticipation of demand; exposes the firm to forecast error and the bullwhip effect
Pull system: production and distribution decisions are triggered by actual customer demand signals; minimizes inventory but requires short lead times and tight information sharing

Push-pull boundary: most supply chains are hybrid, with the upstream portion run as push and the downstream portion run as pull; the location of the boundary is a central design decision (Dell assembled computers to order but held components in vendor-managed inventory)

Strategic fit: the supply chain strategy must match the competitive strategy; misalignment creates what Chopra calls strategic fit zone failure, where cost-focused supply chains support differentiation strategies or responsive supply chains support cost leadership, and the firm suffers on both dimensions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare Walmart versus Zara side by side. For Walmart, map the flows: Procter & Gamble ships truckloads of Tide to Walmart distribution centers, where cross-docking moves the product to store-bound trailers within 24 hours; POS data is shared with P&G via Retail Link so P&G can manage replenishment; the entire chain is engineered to push lowest cost per unit through a pipeline for predictable demand. For Zara, map the flows: designers in Arteixo, Spain finalize garments based on store manager feedback; proprietary factories in Galicia cut and dye fabric that was purchased gray (undyed) and held as inventory; finished garments flow via Zara's central distribution with twice-weekly air shipments; pull signals from stores drive repeat orders within 2 weeks. Compute inventory turns: Walmart turns 8-9 times per year, Zara turns apparel 12 times per year vs traditional retailers at 3-4. Connect each choice back to Fisher's framework: detergent is functional and needs an efficient chain; fashion is innovative and needs a responsive chain.
setup_requirements: Copies of Fisher (1997) HBR article and Chopra Ch. 2
Annual reports or case studies on Walmart, Zara, and Amazon supply chains
Whiteboard for mapping product, information, and financial flows
Spreadsheet for inventory turn and days-of-supply calculations

review.key: Strategic Fit is the Central Question
review.value: A supply chain that minimizes cost is good only if the firm is competing on cost and selling products with predictable demand. Measuring a supply chain against the wrong metrics leads management to destroy the very capabilities customers are paying for

Moving the push-pull boundary upstream reduces inventory and increases responsiveness but demands shorter lead times and better information; Dell's famous direct model pushed the boundary all the way upstream to components, turning finished goods inventory into near zero

The distinction is not about technology sophistication; it refers to variety, life cycle length, and demand predictability. A smartphone is innovative even though it is more advanced than paper towels, and paper towels are functional even though they are in every household

review.key: Three Flows Must All Be Managed
review.value: Most firms think about product flow and neglect information and financial flows, but uncoordinated information causes the bullwhip effect and uncoordinated payment terms strand working capital. Walmart's advantage is as much about Retail Link information sharing as about physical distribution

class_schedule.topic: Demand Forecasting: Qualitative and Quantitative Methods
learning_objectives: Apply time series forecasting techniques including moving average, exponential smoothing, Holt's method, and Winter's method
Decompose a demand series into level, trend, seasonality, and residual components
Compute and interpret MAD, MSE, MAPE, bias, and tracking signal to evaluate forecast quality
Select appropriate qualitative methods (Delphi, sales force composite, executive judgment) when data is insufficient
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 7 - Demand Forecasting in a Supply Chain
Simchi-Levi et al., Designing and Managing the Supply Chain (3rd ed.), Ch. 2
Nahmias, Production and Operations Analysis (7th ed.), Ch. 2
Makridakis, Wheelwright & Hyndman, Forecasting: Methods and Applications (3rd ed.), Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction: Every supply chain decision of consequence depends on a forecast. How many semiconductors should TSMC allocate to automotive customers versus smartphone customers next quarter? How many flu vaccine doses should Sanofi produce before the strain is even confirmed? How many pairs of new Nike Air Force 1s should be placed in 30,000 retail doors for the holiday season? When the forecast is wrong in one direction the firm eats markdowns, excess inventory, and write-downs; when it is wrong in the other direction it loses sales, disappoints customers, and hands market share to competitors who happened to be better prepared. During 2020-2023 the automotive industry experienced a dramatic double forecast error: first underestimating how quickly semiconductor demand would recover after the initial COVID downturn (leading GM, Ford, and Volkswagen to cancel chip orders that were immediately snapped up by consumer electronics makers), then scrambling to get back in line once the recovery accelerated and watching roughly 10.5 million vehicles get cut from global production schedules over two years. A better forecast would have saved those firms tens of billions of dollars. Good forecasting in supply chain is not about finding one magical method that eliminates error; error is unavoidable and aggregates upstream through the bullwhip effect. It is about applying the right method for the data you have, measuring accuracy rigorously, revising as new information arrives, and designing operational hedges (safety stock, flexible capacity, postponement) so the firm is robust when forecasts miss. This session develops the quantitative toolkit and the judgment to apply it.
key_concepts: Components of demand: systematic (level, trend, seasonality) versus random; only the systematic component can be forecast, the random component is irreducible
Static forecasting methods: estimate level, trend, and seasonal factors once from historical data and use them to project forward; simpler but less adaptive
Adaptive forecasting methods: update estimates with each new observation; include moving average, exponential smoothing, Holt's method for trend, Winter's for trend and seasonality
Moving average of n periods: F(t+1) = average of last n actuals; equal weight to recent periods, zero to earlier; lags both trend and seasonality
Simple exponential smoothing: F(t+1) = alpha*D(t) + (1-alpha)*F(t); alpha between 0 and 1 controls responsiveness; alpha near 1 tracks recent changes, alpha near 0 smooths heavily
Holt's method: separately smooths level L(t)=alpha*D(t)+(1-alpha)*(L(t-1)+T(t-1)) and trend T(t)=beta*(L(t)-L(t-1))+(1-beta)*T(t-1); forecast k periods ahead is L(t)+k*T(t)
Winter's method: adds seasonal smoothing S(t)=gamma*(D(t)/L(t))+(1-gamma)*S(t-p) to handle multiplicative seasonality over period p; widely used for retail sales
Forecast error measures: MAD (mean absolute deviation), MSE (mean squared error), MAPE (mean absolute percentage error), bias (sum of error / n, should be near zero)
Tracking signal = running sum of errors / MAD; control limits of plus or minus 4 or 6 flag when a forecast becomes systematically biased
Qualitative methods: Delphi (iterated expert consensus), sales force composite, market research, executive judgment; essential when historical data is missing as in new product launches
lecture_notes.demonstration: Forecast monthly Dell laptop demand from 12 months of sales: 320, 340, 310, 360, 380, 420, 460, 430, 450, 510, 540, 580. Compute 3-month moving average for month 13 = (540+510+580)/3 = 543. Compute exponential smoothing with alpha = 0.3 starting F1 = 320: F(t+1) = 0.3*D(t) + 0.7*F(t), arriving at F13 ~ 428, lagging the upward trend. Apply Holt's with alpha=0.3, beta=0.2: estimate L12 and T12, forecast F13 = L12+T12 ~ 600, capturing the trend. Compute MAD and MAPE for each method to show Holt's outperforms simple smoothing on trending data. Connect to the automotive semiconductor story: firms using stale unadjusted forecasts missed the rebound. Close with a Delphi exercise for a brand-new foldable screen tablet with no history.
setup_requirements: Excel or Google Sheets with forecasting functions and charting
Historical demand data sets (Dell laptops, Nike shoes, US flu vaccine demand)
Reference card with forecasting formulas and smoothing constant guidelines
Access to forecasting software (Forecast Pro, R forecast package, or Python statsmodels)

review.key: Match the Method to the Pattern
review.value: Applying simple exponential smoothing to strongly trending data guarantees a lagging forecast; applying Holt's to stationary data adds noise. Always plot the data first and identify level, trend, and seasonality before choosing a method

The question is not whether a forecast is right, it is whether its errors are small enough and unbiased enough to make decisions. A 10% MAPE forecast with stable accuracy beats a 5% MAPE forecast that suddenly breaks
Forecast accuracy improves as you aggregate across SKUs, stores, or time periods; supply chains rely on rolled-up forecasts for capacity planning and disaggregate only for replenishment
A forecast with zero bias but high variance is honest; a forecast with persistent bias leads to systematic over-stocking or stock-outs. Tracking signal catches bias before it becomes catastrophic

class_schedule.topic: Aggregate Planning and Sales and Operations Planning
learning_objectives: Formulate aggregate planning problems that balance demand and capacity over a 3 to 18 month horizon
Compare chase, level, and mixed strategies and compute their cost implications
Set up aggregate planning as a linear program with variables for production, workforce, overtime, and inventory
Explain the Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP) process and its role in aligning commercial and operational plans
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 8 - Aggregate Planning
Jacobs & Chase, Operations and Supply Chain Management (16th ed.), Ch. 19
Wallace & Stahl, Sales and Operations Planning: The How-To Handbook (3rd ed.), Ch. 1-3
Nahmias, Production and Operations Analysis (7th ed.), Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Aggregate planning is the process of determining the quantity and timing of production over an intermediate horizon, typically 3 to 18 months, matching supply to demand while minimizing cost. It sits between strategic capacity planning (which fixes factories and permanent workforce for years) and operational scheduling (which sequences specific jobs next week). At the aggregate level you decide how many total units to produce per month, how many workers to employ, how much overtime to run, how much to subcontract, and how much inventory to build or draw down. These decisions commit the firm to costs that cannot easily be reversed: hiring and training costs, layoff severance, inventory holding expense, overtime premiums, backorder penalties, subcontracting contracts. Done well, aggregate planning lets a firm ride out seasonal swings and absorb forecast errors without panic. Done poorly, it leaves the firm either hiring and firing in waves or paying huge inventory and backlog bills. Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP) is the monthly management process that makes aggregate planning work in a large organization, bringing together sales, marketing, operations, finance, and executives around a single consensus plan. Pre-S&OP, firms ran what Tom Wallace called 'dueling forecasts,' where sales projected optimistic numbers to motivate the field and operations planned on conservative numbers. S&OP replaces the duel with one number, a practice now standard at Procter & Gamble, Colgate, Unilever, and John Deere. The 2020-2022 disruption cycle rediscovered S&OP for an entire generation of executives who had let their processes degrade during the long period of stable demand that preceded it.
key_concepts: Aggregate planning horizon: 3 to 18 months, bridges strategic capacity and short term scheduling; expressed in aggregate units across a product family rather than specific SKUs
Decision variables: regular time production, overtime production, subcontracted units, hires and layoffs each period, ending inventory, backorders

Cost components: regular wages, overtime premium (typically 1.5x), inventory holding cost per unit per period, backorder cost per unit, hiring cost per worker, layoff cost per worker, subcontracting cost per unit
Chase strategy: match production to demand by varying workforce through hiring and firing; minimizes inventory but creates turbulence and morale issues
Level strategy: maintain constant workforce and constant output, absorbing demand variation through inventory and backorders; smooths labor but builds costly seasonal inventory
Mixed strategy: level base workforce with overtime and subcontracting to handle peaks; almost always cheaper than pure strategies in realistic cost structures

Linear programming formulation: minimize total cost subject to inventory balance, workforce balance, demand satisfaction, and capacity constraints; solvable with Excel Solver at thousands of variables
S&OP five-step process: data gathering, demand planning, supply planning, pre-S&OP meeting, executive S&OP meeting; produces a single agreed plan covering 18 months
One number philosophy: the whole firm commits to the same demand forecast, operations plan, and financial plan; shatters the old pattern of incompatible plans across functions
Integrated Business Planning (IBP): evolution of S&OP that adds financial gap closure and strategic linkage, pushing horizon to 24-36 months
lecture_notes.demonstration: Solve a quarterly aggregate plan for a lawnmower company. Forecast: Q1=1500, Q2=3500, Q3=3000, Q4=1000 units. Regular capacity 2000/quarter at $40, overtime up to 500 at $60, subcontract up to 1000 at $75, holding $5/unit/quarter, backorder $20/unit. Chase strategy: produce exactly to demand using overtime and subcontract for Q2 and Q3 overflow. Level: produce 2250/quarter constant, build inventory in Q1 and Q4, draw down in Q2 and Q3. Mixed: run regular time at capacity, add overtime in Q2 and Q3, modest inventory buildup in Q1. Compute total costs for each and identify the winner. Sketch the LP with variables P(t), O(t), S(t), I(t), B(t) and inventory balance I(t) = I(t-1) + P(t) + O(t) + S(t) - D(t). Link to S&OP at John Deere: who owns each number, how often it updates, what happens when sales and operations disagree.
setup_requirements: Excel with Solver add-in enabled for LP models
Cost and capacity data for the lawnmower case
S&OP process templates from Wallace & Stahl
Case study: 'Sport Obermeyer' (HBS 9-695-022)

review.value: Pure chase or pure level rarely minimize cost; real cost structures penalize both hiring turbulence and large inventory holdings. The optimal plan usually combines a stable core workforce with flexible overtime, subcontracting, and modest inventory buffers

review.key: Hiring and Firing is Expensive and Slow
review.value: Textbook chase strategies assume frictionless labor, but hiring costs thousands per worker, training takes weeks, and layoffs damage morale. These costs usually dominate the holding cost of building inventory

The single biggest benefit of S&OP is eliminating competing forecasts; when sales, operations, and finance commit to one plan the firm stops wasting effort on reconciliation and starts managing the actual gap between plan and reality

In an LP, the binding constraints tell you where to invest; if overtime is always binding then more regular capacity or outsourcing has positive expected value, and the LP's dual prices quantify how much

class_schedule.topic: Inventory Management I: EOQ, Reorder Points, and Safety Stock
learning_objectives: Derive and apply the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) model and understand its sensitivity to parameter changes
Compute reorder points under constant and stochastic lead time demand
Set safety stock levels to achieve a specified cycle service level using normal distribution z-scores
Calculate expected inventory cost and identify the trade-off between cycle stock and ordering cost
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 12-13
Nahmias, Production and Operations Analysis (7th ed.), Ch. 4-5
Silver, Pyke & Thomas, Inventory and Production Management in Supply Chains (4th ed.), Ch. 5-7
Harris, 'How Many Parts to Make at Once,' Factory, The Magazine of Management (1913)

lecture_notes.introduction: Inventory is the lifeblood and the curse of every supply chain. It absorbs the mismatch between supply and demand, between production speed and purchase speed, between replenishment lead time and order cycles. Without inventory, a single delayed shipment would starve downstream stages and halt production. With too much inventory, working capital is tied up, warehousing costs balloon, obsolescence risk soars, and the firm loses flexibility to respond to new opportunities. Walmart carries roughly $56 billion of inventory at any moment across its stores and DCs, representing about 46 days of supply; Amazon carries around $35 billion and turns it faster. Each day of inventory reduction at Walmart scale saves hundreds of millions in holding costs and frees up billions in working capital, which is why Walmart has invested so heavily in cross-docking, vendor-managed inventory, and demand-driven replenishment. On the other end, Boeing's 787 program discovered that pushing inventory down to suppliers without managing the overall chain led to mass shortages when multiple tier-two suppliers failed simultaneously, grounding a $32 billion program. This session develops the foundational quantitative models of inventory management: EOQ that balances ordering cost against holding cost, reorder points that trigger replenishment, and safety stock that buffers against variability. These models were first published by Ford Whitman Harris at Westinghouse in 1913 and remain the backbone of inventory practice, embedded in every ERP system from SAP to Oracle to NetSuite. Understanding them from the ground up matters because parameter choices (ordering cost, holding cost rate, service level target) drive the results, and those parameters are almost always wrong if chosen without discipline.
key_concepts: Cycle inventory: inventory held to satisfy demand between replenishment orders; equals Q/2 on average under EOQ assumptions
Safety inventory: inventory held to buffer against uncertainty in demand and lead time; sized to achieve a specified service level
Seasonal inventory: built up in advance of known seasonal peaks to smooth production; managed in aggregate planning rather than EOQ
Pipeline inventory: inventory in transit between stages; equals lead time times average demand rate (Little's Law)
EOQ formula: Q* = sqrt(2*D*S/H) where D is annual demand, S is fixed cost per order, H is annual holding cost per unit; derives from minimizing total cost = D*S/Q + H*Q/2

EOQ assumptions: constant demand, constant lead time, no stockouts, fixed ordering cost, constant unit cost; robust because the total cost curve is flat near Q*, so 50% errors in Q produce only 6% error in cost
Reorder point: ROP = d*L under deterministic demand; triggers an order when inventory reaches this level
Safety stock under normal demand: SS = z * sigma_L where sigma_L is standard deviation of lead-time demand and z is the z-score for target cycle service level (z=1.65 for 95%, z=2.33 for 99%)
Lead-time demand std dev: sigma_L = sigma_d * sqrt(L) when only demand varies; sigma_L = sqrt(L*sigma_d^2 + d^2*sigma_L^2) when lead time also varies
Cycle service level vs fill rate: CSL is probability of no stockout in a cycle; fill rate is fraction of demand met from stock; fill rate is usually higher than CSL for the same safety stock
lecture_notes.demonstration: Optimal inventory policy for a Dell DC stocking 27-inch monitors. Annual demand D = 12,000 units, ordering cost S = $100, unit cost c = $200, holding cost rate h = 25% so H = $50 per unit per year. EOQ = sqrt(2*12000*100/50) = sqrt(48000) = 219 units. Orders per year = 54.8. Average cycle inventory = 110. Ordering cost = $5480, holding cost = $5500 (approximately equal at optimum, confirming the formula). Add uncertainty: daily demand mean d = 33, sigma_d = 12, lead time L = 5 days. Lead-time demand mean = 165, std dev = 12*sqrt(5) = 26.8. For 95% service level use z = 1.65, safety stock = 44 units, ROP = 165 + 44 = 209. For 99% use z = 2.33, safety stock = 62 units. Highlight the disproportionate cost of going from 95% to 99%. Walk through the Harris (1913) derivation to show where the square root comes from, then discuss why many firms over-inflate ordering cost parameters and end up ordering in batches far larger than optimal.
setup_requirements: EOQ spreadsheet template with sensitivity analysis
Normal distribution z-table for service levels from 80% to 99.99%
Real demand and lead time data (Dell monitors or Amazon books)
SAP or Oracle screen showing MRP reorder point configuration

review.key: EOQ is Robust to Parameter Errors
review.value: Because the total cost curve is flat near the optimum, a 50% error in D or S produces only a 6% error in total cost; do not agonize over precise parameter estimates, but avoid order-of-magnitude errors
Going from 95% to 99% service level requires roughly 41% more safety stock, and going from 99% to 99.9% requires another 33%; these increments become dramatically expensive for fast-moving SKUs

review.key: Safety Stock is a Function of Lead Time
review.value: Safety stock grows with the square root of lead time; cutting lead time in half reduces required safety stock by about 29%, often the fastest way to reduce inventory without touching service levels

review.key: Holding Cost Rate is Higher Than Accounting Suggests
review.value: The textbook 25-35% annual holding cost covers capital cost, storage, obsolescence, shrinkage, and insurance; firms that use only capital cost understate the true cost and carry too much inventory

class_schedule.topic: Inventory Management II: Multi-echelon, Newsvendor, and Periodic Review
learning_objectives: Solve the newsvendor problem using the critical ratio Cu/(Cu+Co) and normal demand assumptions
Implement periodic review (R,S) and continuous review (s,Q) policies and compare their safety stock requirements
Explain multi-echelon inventory concepts including echelon inventory, installation stock, and risk pooling
Apply the square root law of inventory aggregation to evaluate centralization decisions
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 13-14
Simchi-Levi et al., Designing and Managing the Supply Chain (3rd ed.), Ch. 3
Zipkin, Foundations of Inventory Management, Ch. 6-9
Eeckhoudt, Gollier & Schlesinger, 'The Risk-Averse (and Prudent) Newsboy,' Management Science (1995)

lecture_notes.introduction: The basic EOQ model assumes constant demand, constant lead time, and infinite planning horizons. Real supply chains violate all three assumptions constantly. Consider a ski parka buyer at L.L. Bean making a single order decision for the winter season: no reorders are possible after the supplier's production run, demand is uncertain, unsold units must be marked down or carried to next year, and stockouts lose margin. This is the newsvendor problem, named after the corner newspaper vendor who must decide how many papers to buy each morning knowing unsold copies are worthless by evening. It is the canonical single-period inventory problem and governs decisions from fashion purchasing to vaccine production to restaurant menu planning. Marshall Fisher and Ananth Raman studied Sport Obermeyer, a ski apparel company, and showed that newsvendor logic combined with early-season market signals could cut forecast error in half and dramatically improve both stockouts and markdowns. Another family of models handles periodic review systems where inventory is checked at fixed intervals and an order is placed to bring inventory up to a target level; this is standard in pharmacies, hospital stockrooms, and retail back rooms. Finally, real supply chains have multiple stages (supplier, distributor, retailer) and inventory at one stage affects decisions at others. Multi-echelon models handle these interactions and enable decisions such as whether to centralize safety stock at a regional DC or decentralize at local stores; the answer turns on the square root law of aggregation, which shows that pooling N independent demand sources requires safety stock growing only with sqrt(N) rather than N. This is why Amazon can hold fewer total days of supply than a traditional retailer with store-level stocking.
key_concepts: Newsvendor problem: single-period stocking with uncertain demand, overage cost Co, underage cost Cu; optimal Q* satisfies F(Q*) = Cu/(Cu+Co)
Critical ratio interpretation: Cu/(Cu+Co) is the fraction of the demand distribution to cover; high margins push toward more stock, high disposal costs push toward less
Normal demand newsvendor: Q* = mu + z*sigma where z is the inverse normal CDF at the critical ratio
Expected profit at Q*: mu*margin minus expected shortage*Cu minus expected leftover*Co; requires normal loss function tables
Continuous review (s,Q) policy: place a fixed-size order Q whenever inventory reaches reorder point s; assumes continuous inventory monitoring (scanners, ERP)
Periodic review (R,S) policy: check inventory every R periods and order up to level S; safety stock must cover demand variability over R+L rather than just L
Base-stock level for periodic review: S = d*(R+L) + z*sigma*sqrt(R+L); sqrt(R+L) shows how review period inflates the risk horizon
Echelon inventory: inventory at a stage plus all inventory downstream; the correct unit for multi-echelon safety stock accounting (Clark & Scarf, 1960)
Risk pooling: aggregating uncertain demands reduces relative variability; for N identical independent sources, total sigma scales as sigma*sqrt(N) instead of N*sigma

Square root law of inventory: total safety stock across N identical locations can be reduced to sqrt(N) times the single-location safety stock if demand is pooled; for N=4 this is 50% less, for N=9 it is 67% less
lecture_notes.demonstration: Newsvendor calculation for L.L. Bean ski parkas. Parka cost = $50, selling price = $120, markdown salvage = $30. Cu = 120 - 50 = $70 per lost sale, Co = 50 - 30 = $20 per leftover unit. Critical ratio = 70/(70+20) = 0.778. Demand normal with mu = 2000, sigma = 400. From normal tables, z for 0.778 is 0.765. Optimal Q* = 2000 + 0.765*400 = 2306 parkas. Then apply the square root law: Bean has 12 regional warehouses, each facing independent demand mean 2000 and sigma 400, holding safety stock at 95% of 1.65*400 = 660 per location, totaling 7920 units. If centralized, pooled demand has mean 24000 and sigma = 400*sqrt(12) = 1386, so safety stock = 1.65*1386 = 2287. Saving = 5633 units, about 71% less safety stock for the same service level. Discuss the trade-off: centralization saves inventory but increases outbound transportation cost and service time. Show how Amazon solves the trade-off with many regional fulfillment centers and same-day delivery.
setup_requirements: Newsvendor spreadsheet with normal loss function tables
Sport Obermeyer case (HBS 9-695-022) for newsvendor application
Historical demand data with mean and standard deviation for multiple SKUs
Map of Amazon or L.L. Bean distribution network for risk pooling discussion

review.value: The newsvendor optimum is set entirely by the ratio Cu/(Cu+Co); high margin products should be stocked aggressively past mean demand, low margin products should be stocked below mean demand

review.key: Periodic Review Needs More Safety Stock
review.value: A periodic review system with review period R must carry safety stock for R+L periods of uncertainty, not just L; this penalty is the price paid for the simplicity of fixed review times

Centralizing inventory reduces total safety stock without reducing service level, one of the few supply chain decisions with a clear win; the catch is that centralization may increase transportation cost or response time

Delaying product differentiation (painting, packaging, customization) until after demand information arrives lets the firm pool uncertainty across variants; HP redesigned DeskJet printers to postpone power supply configuration, cutting safety stock by about 25%

class_schedule.topic: Distribution Network Design and Facility Location
learning_objectives: Compare distribution network designs: direct ship, warehousing, cross-docking, and hub-and-spoke
Evaluate facility location decisions using center of gravity, weighted factor rating, and mixed integer programming
Analyze trade-offs between inbound transportation, outbound transportation, inventory, and facility costs
Understand how e-commerce has reshaped last-mile network design
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 4-5
Ballou, Business Logistics / Supply Chain Management (5th ed.), Ch. 13-14
Simchi-Levi et al., Designing and Managing the Supply Chain (3rd ed.), Ch. 4
Daskin, Network and Discrete Location: Models, Algorithms, and Applications, Ch. 2-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Where you put warehouses, how many to build, and how products flow through them are decisions that lock in billions of dollars of fixed investment and determine service levels for decades. Amazon's fulfillment network now includes more than 175 fulfillment centers, 150 sortation centers, and thousands of delivery stations around the world, each choice driven by population density, labor availability, tax incentives, and the economics of next-day and same-day delivery. When Amazon opened its first sortation center in Phoenix in 2014, the logic was simple: by presorting packages in the middle mile rather than at the USPS hub, Amazon could capture Sunday delivery and 7-day-a-week coverage that the traditional retailers could not match. Walmart's distribution network is engineered for a different purpose: 42 regional distribution centers each serving 90 to 100 stores within a 150-mile radius, using cross-docking so that product flows from inbound trailer to outbound trailer within 24 hours without being put to stock. This design minimizes inventory across the network at the cost of requiring very close supplier coordination and high outbound frequency. In contrast, a European brewer such as Heineken runs a completely different network driven by the weight-to-value ratio of beer: breweries located near population centers to minimize outbound ton-miles, and modest finished goods inventory because the product is stable. The session develops the quantitative tools for evaluating these choices, including the classic center of gravity method, factor rating for qualitative comparisons, and mixed integer programming for optimal facility selection among many candidate sites. The critical insight is that the best network depends on the cost structure: if transportation dominates, minimize distance; if inventory dominates, centralize; if service time is critical, decentralize with more smaller facilities.
key_concepts: Manufacturer storage with direct shipping: manufacturer ships directly to customer, bypassing retailer warehouses; works for high-variety, low-volume items (Dell computers, custom furniture)
Manufacturer storage with in-transit merge: multiple shipments combined in transit; used for bulky items shipped separately
Distributor storage with package carrier delivery: intermediate warehouses hold inventory and ship via UPS/FedEx; Amazon's traditional model
Distributor storage with last-mile delivery: the distributor owns the last-mile vehicle (Amazon Flex, grocery delivery vans); enables same-day delivery at higher cost
Retail storage with customer pickup: inventory sits in retail stores, customer picks up (BOPIS - buy online pick up in store); leverages existing retail footprint
Cross-docking: product is received at a facility and immediately moved to outbound without being put to stock; dramatically reduces inventory and handling cost but requires tight coordination
Center of gravity method: optimal single facility location minimizes weighted transportation cost; x* = sum(wi*xi)/sum(wi), y* = sum(wi*yi)/sum(wi); simple and useful as a starting point
Weighted factor rating: evaluates candidate sites on multiple weighted criteria (labor cost, tax incentives, proximity to customers, infrastructure); produces comparative scores

Mixed integer programming for facility location: binary variables for open/close, continuous variables for flow; minimize fixed facility cost plus transportation cost subject to demand and capacity constraints
Total landed cost: the full delivered cost including product, inbound freight, duties, handling, inventory carrying, and outbound freight; the correct metric for network design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply the center of gravity method to locate a new distribution center serving four regional markets: Atlanta (demand=300 tons/week, coordinates 84W 34N), Dallas (demand=500, 97W 33N), Denver (demand=200, 105W 40N), Chicago (demand=400, 88W 42N). Compute the weighted center: x* = (300*84 + 500*97 + 200*105 + 400*88)/(300+500+200+400) = 132100/1400 = 94.4W, y* = (300*34 + 500*33 + 200*40 + 400*42)/1400 = 49500/1400 = 35.4N. That puts the optimal point near Little Rock, Arkansas or Memphis, which is exactly where FedEx has major hubs. Then extend to a mixed integer programming problem with three candidate sites (Memphis, Dallas, Kansas City) and fixed costs per site; formulate in Excel Solver and solve for the optimal combination. Close by comparing Walmart's DC network (42 centers, each 1-2 million sq ft, 150 mile radius) to Amazon's fulfillment network (175+ FCs with many smaller last-mile facilities) and explaining the different cost structures that make each optimal.
setup_requirements: Excel with Solver for MIP facility location
Geographic data set with demand points and candidate facility locations
Cost data: fixed facility costs, transportation rates per ton-mile, inventory carrying rates
Maps of Walmart DC network and Amazon fulfillment network for comparison

review.value: More facilities means higher facility cost and inventory cost but lower outbound transportation and response time; fewer facilities means the reverse. The optimal number depends on cost structure and service targets, not on a universal rule

review.key: Center of Gravity is a Starting Point Only
review.value: The method assumes Euclidean distance and ignores actual roads, ports, labor markets, and tax incentives; it produces a useful initial location that must be refined with real constraints

Cross-docking slashes inventory and handling costs but only works when inbound and outbound shipments are tightly synchronized; misalignment turns a cross-dock into an expensive warehouse with no storage capacity
Traditional networks optimized for case pick to stores; e-commerce requires piece pick to homes with far more touches per unit, shifting the optimal network toward more facilities closer to customers

learning_objectives: Compare transportation modes (truck, rail, air, ocean, pipeline, intermodal) on speed, cost, reliability, and capacity
Calculate transportation cost per unit and per ton-mile for alternative mode and routing decisions
Apply the economic transportation decision framework including inventory-in-transit costs and lead-time effects
Understand the last-mile delivery challenge and its implications for e-commerce economics
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 14
Mangan, Lalwani & Butcher, Global Logistics and Supply Chain Management (4th ed.), Ch. 5-6
Coyle, Novack & Gibson, Transportation: A Supply Chain Perspective (8th ed.), Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Transportation moves roughly 75% of everything you own at some point in its life. The US spent approximately $1.36 trillion on freight transportation in 2022, about 6% of GDP. The transportation mode decision is one of the most consequential supply chain choices because modes differ by a factor of 20x in cost per ton-mile and by weeks in transit time. Ocean container shipping costs around $0.005-0.02 per ton-mile and takes 4-6 weeks from Shanghai to Los Angeles. Air freight costs $0.50-1.00 per ton-mile and delivers in 1-3 days. Rail costs around $0.03 per ton-mile but only serves rail-connected origins and destinations. Truck is the US workhorse at $0.12-0.20 per ton-mile for full truckload, flexible door-to-door, with about 72% of all US freight by value. Intermodal combines truck and rail to approach rail economics while preserving some truck flexibility. The right mode depends not only on direct transportation cost but on inventory-in-transit cost (capital tied up in goods while they are moving), service requirements, and the cost of stockouts or delays. A $1000 laptop moving from Shenzhen to Memphis ties up about $3 per day of capital per unit; 30 days of ocean transit versus 3 days of air means $81 more in inventory carrying cost, which may easily justify the air premium for high-value goods. The session covers the mode selection framework, intermodal decisions, and the last-mile problem which has become the hardest part of the chain as e-commerce has grown. Amazon spent over $90 billion on shipping costs in 2023, more than any retailer in history, and it is still losing money on many small-package home deliveries.
key_concepts: Truckload (TL): full trailer of 40,000-45,000 lbs; cheapest per unit for full loads but inflexible on partial loads; cost approximately $1.50-2.50 per mile
Less-than-truckload (LTL): multiple shippers share a trailer; more expensive per unit than TL but economical for partial loads; hub-and-spoke networks (Old Dominion, XPO, Estes)
Rail: economical for heavy bulky low-value commodities over long distances (coal, grain, chemicals); 3-4x cheaper per ton-mile than truck but slower and less flexible

Intermodal (TOFC/COFC): truck trailers or containers on rail cars; combines rail economics for long-haul with truck flexibility for first and last mile; BNSF, Union Pacific, and J.B. Hunt run major intermodal services
Air cargo: 10-30x more expensive than ocean per ton-mile but 10x faster; essential for high-value, perishable, or time-critical goods (pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, fresh flowers)

Ocean container shipping: the backbone of global trade, about 80% of goods by volume; dominated by alliances (2M Maersk/MSC, Ocean Alliance CMA-CGM/COSCO/Evergreen, THE Alliance Hapag-Lloyd/ONE/Yang Ming/HMM)
Pipeline: lowest cost per ton-mile for liquids and gases; major pipelines for oil, natural gas, refined products; limited to network coverage
Inventory-in-transit cost: pipeline inventory = daily demand * transit time; holding cost applied to this inventory; a key trade-off against faster modes
Last-mile delivery: the most expensive leg of the chain, often 28-53% of total delivery cost; driven by stop density, package size, and failed delivery rates
Hours of service regulations: US DOT limits truck drivers to 11 hours driving in a 14-hour window with mandatory 10-hour rest; affects transit times and labor requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluate the mode decision for shipping 40 containers of Samsung smartphones from Incheon, Korea to Dallas, Texas. Ocean: transit 18 days, cost $4,500 per container total landed, inventory-in-transit cost = $500,000 per container * 18/365 * 0.25 holding rate = $6,164 per container, total $10,664. Air: transit 2 days, cost $45,000 per container, inventory-in-transit cost = $500,000 * 2/365 * 0.25 = $685, total $45,685. Simple comparison says ocean wins at $10,664 vs $45,685. But add the stockout risk: if 18-day transit causes a 5% probability of missing a product launch window with $2M revenue loss per container, expected stockout cost = $100,000 per container, tipping the balance strongly toward air for launch inventory while ocean remains best for replenishment. Show how Apple splits iPhone launches between air (initial launch volume for retail stores) and ocean (replenishment for the weeks that follow). Then compute last-mile cost comparisons: Amazon Flex at $20-25 per hour making 20-30 stops, FedEx at $10-15 per package, USPS at $3-5 per package for small items; explain why Amazon built its own delivery network.
setup_requirements: Transportation rate sheets for TL, LTL, intermodal, air, ocean
Distance tables and transit time databases (PC Miler, Google Maps API)
Case study: 'Apple iPhone Launch Supply Chain' for air vs ocean split
Historical freight cost data showing 2021-2022 container rate spikes

review.key: Mode Decision is Total Cost, Not Freight Cost
review.value: Freight cost alone usually recommends ocean or rail, but adding inventory-in-transit and stockout costs can flip the decision toward air or truck. Always compute the full cost including inventory effects

review.key: Intermodal Captures Most of Rail Economics
review.value: For cross-country US moves, intermodal costs 15-30% less than over-the-road truck with only 1-2 days added transit; it is the default for non-time-critical long-haul freight and is why J.B. Hunt built its intermodal franchise

Middle-mile costs have been optimized for decades, but last-mile delivery is expensive and still evolving; Amazon, FedEx, and UPS are competing on technology (routing algorithms, delivery drones, lockers) to crack the last-mile cost problem

review.key: Ocean Shipping is a Concentrated Market
review.value: Three alliances control over 80% of global container capacity; the 2016 Hanjin bankruptcy, the 2021 Suez blockage, and 2021-2022 rate spikes demonstrated how fragile this concentration can be when shocks arrive

learning_objectives: Apply the Kraljic portfolio matrix to classify purchased items by risk and financial impact
Decide when to use competitive bidding versus negotiated sourcing versus long-term partnerships
Compute total cost of ownership (TCO) including price, quality, delivery, and service costs
Analyze make versus buy decisions and identify strategic sourcing implications
readings: Monczka, Handfield, Giunipero & Patterson, Purchasing and Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 1-6
Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 15
Kraljic, 'Purchasing Must Become Supply Management,' Harvard Business Review (September 1983)
Porter, Competitive Strategy, Ch. 6 - Strategic Analysis of Vertical Integration

lecture_notes.introduction: Procurement spending is usually the largest cost category for manufacturing firms, running 50-70% of revenue for most industries, which means a 2% savings on purchased goods drops more to the bottom line than a 10% increase in sales. Yet for decades procurement was treated as a tactical clerical function rather than a strategic lever, until Peter Kraljic's 1983 HBR article argued that purchasing should become supply management and gave managers a framework for differentiating the treatment of different spend categories. The Kraljic matrix classifies purchases on two dimensions: financial impact (the spend amount) and supply risk (availability, number of suppliers, substitutability, switching costs). The four resulting quadrants each call for different sourcing strategies. Non-critical items such as office supplies warrant simple competitive bidding and automated purchasing. Leverage items such as standard components where there are many qualified suppliers and high spend call for aggressive negotiation and supplier consolidation. Bottleneck items with few suppliers but moderate spend require assured supply through contracts, inventory, or multi-sourcing. Strategic items with both high spend and high supply risk (think Boeing's relationship with RTX for jet engines or Apple's relationship with TSMC for chips) call for long-term partnerships, deep technical collaboration, and joint investment. Getting these categorizations wrong is expensive: treating a strategic partner like a commodity supplier destroys the partnership, and treating a commodity like a strategic partner wastes enormous resources. Apple's famously aggressive procurement practices, where senior VPs personally renegotiate contracts with key suppliers every year, reflect a strategic supply chain discipline that enables the thinnest finished products and the fastest ramps in electronics history. This session develops the sourcing framework, the TCO discipline, and the critical make-versus-buy decision that shapes corporate boundaries.
key_concepts: Kraljic matrix: classify items by financial impact (high/low) and supply risk (high/low); four categories non-critical, leverage, bottleneck, strategic each with different optimal sourcing strategies
Non-critical items (low impact, low risk): automate with e-procurement, blanket purchase orders, purchasing cards; minimize transaction cost
Leverage items (high impact, low risk): use volume leverage, competitive bidding, supplier consolidation, reverse auctions; maximize cost savings
Bottleneck items (low impact, high risk): ensure supply through contracts, safety stock, alternate sources; manage risk
Strategic items (high impact, high risk): develop deep supplier partnerships, joint investment, long-term contracts, information sharing; create competitive advantage through supplier capability

Total cost of ownership (TCO): price is only part of the cost; add quality (defect rates, inspection cost), delivery (late penalties, expediting), service (technical support, returns), and lifecycle (maintenance, disposal)
Competitive bidding: formal RFP to multiple qualified suppliers, selection on lowest responsive bid; works for well-specified items with many capable suppliers
Negotiated sourcing: direct negotiation with one or a few suppliers; needed when specifications are complex, relationships matter, or the supply base is narrow

Make vs buy decision: analyze whether an activity should be performed in-house or outsourced; factors include cost, quality control, intellectual property, capacity utilization, and strategic importance

Insourcing and reshoring: the 2015-present reversal of decades of offshoring, driven by rising labor costs, tariffs, shipping volatility, automation, and geopolitical risk; TSMC building in Arizona, Intel in Ohio, GM and Ford bringing battery cell production back to the US
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply the Kraljic matrix to Toyota's procurement portfolio. Non-critical: office supplies, factory consumables - automated e-procurement through Ariba. Leverage: standard steel, commodity fasteners, tires - competitive bidding with 3-5 qualified suppliers, aggressive price pressure. Bottleneck: specialty rare earth magnets for hybrid motors, certain pigments for paint - dual-sourced with safety stock and long-term contracts. Strategic: electric vehicle batteries, advanced semiconductors, custom dashboard electronics - deep partnerships with Panasonic (batteries), Denso (electronics), and TSMC (chips), with joint R&D and long horizons. Then compute a TCO comparison between two potential suppliers of a dashboard electronics module. Supplier A: unit price $85, defect rate 500 ppm, average delivery lateness 0.5 days. Supplier B: unit price $92, defect rate 100 ppm, average delivery lateness 0 days. Assuming quality cost of $200 per defect and lateness cost of $50 per day of lateness per order, compute TCO A = $85 + 200*(500/1000000) + 50*0.5/order volume vs TCO B = $92 + 200*(100/1000000) + 0 = $92.02. With small volumes B wins; with large volumes A's quality penalty grows until B wins decisively. Discuss why auto OEMs often pay more for proven suppliers rather than chase the lowest price.
setup_requirements: Kraljic matrix template with examples from multiple industries
TCO calculation spreadsheet with quality, delivery, and service cost inputs
Case study: 'Apple and Its Suppliers' (HBS 9-712-456)
Sample RFP templates for different item categories

review.key: Not Every Supplier Needs a Partnership
review.value: Deep partnerships are expensive in management attention and commitment; reserve them for strategic items where capability and collaboration generate competitive advantage. Commodity items should be sourced competitively without sentiment
The cheapest unit price often hides enormous quality, delivery, and service costs; TCO discipline is what separates mature procurement organizations from ones that get blindsided by hidden costs

review.key: Make vs Buy is Rarely Permanent
review.value: Firms outsource and insource on multi-year cycles as cost structures shift; Apple outsources manufacturing but insources chip design, and Tesla has insourced battery cells while outsourcing others. The decision should be revisited periodically

Reshoring raises direct labor costs in almost every case but reduces supply risk, tariff exposure, and lead time variability; the recent wave reflects geopolitical and resilience concerns, not cost savings

learning_objectives: Design supply contracts that align incentives between buyer and supplier (buyback, revenue sharing, quantity flexibility)
Evaluate supplier performance using scorecards and integrated metrics
Understand the bullwhip effect's origin in contract terms and the role of contracts in supply chain coordination
Implement supplier development programs and manage strategic alliances
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 15-16
Monczka et al., Purchasing and Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 7-10
Cachon, 'Supply Chain Coordination with Contracts,' Handbook in OR & MS, Vol. 11 (2003)
Liker & Choi, 'Building Deep Supplier Relationships,' Harvard Business Review (December 2004)

lecture_notes.introduction: Supply chain contracts are the legal and economic glue that holds multi-party supply chains together, and their design has profound effects on how the chain performs. The classic problem is double marginalization: when each stage in the chain independently chooses quantities to maximize its own profit, the total chain profit is lower than if a single decision maker controlled everything. A wholesale price contract (the buyer pays a fixed per-unit wholesale price) creates this problem because the retailer orders less than the chain-optimal quantity, since the retailer bears all the risk of unsold inventory. The contract design literature, led by Gerard Cachon and others, has shown that cleverer contract forms can align incentives and recover the lost profit. Buyback contracts, where the manufacturer agrees to buy back unsold units at a fraction of the wholesale price, shift some stockout risk back upstream; Warner Brothers and Disney use them routinely for retail DVD sales. Revenue-sharing contracts, where the retailer pays a lower up-front wholesale price but shares a percentage of final revenue, were famously implemented by Blockbuster and Hollywood studios in the late 1990s, cutting Blockbuster's per-tape cost from $60-70 to about $8 in exchange for 30-45% of rental revenue, which dramatically improved availability and profits for both sides. Quantity flexibility contracts allow the buyer to adjust orders within a window, absorbing some demand risk for the supplier. Beyond contracts, the relationship itself matters: Toyota's keiretsu system, where the automaker owns equity in key suppliers and provides engineering support, has delivered decades of cost reduction and quality improvement that arms-length relationships cannot match. Liker and Choi's 'partnering hierarchy' from their HBR study of Toyota and Honda shows how deep supplier relationships are built through mutual understanding, trust, shared information, and joint improvement activities. Compare this to the 1990s General Motors strategy under purchasing czar Ignacio Lopez, who slashed supplier prices unilaterally and destroyed much of GM's supplier cooperation for a generation. The point is that contract mechanics and relationship quality are both levers of performance, and the best supply chains master both.
key_concepts: Double marginalization: when each stage in a serial supply chain marks up independently, the combined markup shrinks total demand and profit below the integrated optimum
Wholesale price contract: simple fixed unit price; baseline contract that suffers from double marginalization; easy to administer, coordinating only under specific conditions

Buyback contract: manufacturer buys back unsold units at a fraction of wholesale price; shifts inventory risk upstream and allows the retailer to stock more, improving total chain profit; widely used in book publishing

Revenue-sharing contract: lower wholesale price combined with revenue share to the manufacturer; transforms the retailer's decision and aligns incentives; Blockbuster-Hollywood case is the textbook example
Quantity flexibility contract: the buyer commits to a base quantity with the right to adjust up or down within a window; useful when demand is uncertain and lead times are long
Supplier scorecard: multi-dimensional performance tracking (quality ppm, on-time delivery percentage, cost reduction, responsiveness, innovation); drives behavior through measurement
Toyota keiretsu: vertically coordinated network of suppliers with cross-equity ownership, joint R&D, and long-term commitment; contrasts with arms-length US automaker relationships historically

Liker and Choi partnering hierarchy: mutual understanding, develop suppliers (training, information sharing), share information, joint improvement activities, supervise; each level builds on the previous

Supplier development: formal programs to help suppliers improve quality, reduce cost, or adopt new technology; Toyota's TSSC (Toyota Supplier Support Center) has coached hundreds of suppliers in lean manufacturing
Strategic alliance: long-term commitment beyond a normal contract including shared goals, joint investment, and information sharing; creates switching costs for both parties
lecture_notes.demonstration: Numerical analysis of Blockbuster-Hollywood revenue sharing. Under the old wholesale contract, Blockbuster paid $65 per VHS tape and kept 100% of rental revenue. At a typical $3 rental rate, Blockbuster needed 22 rentals per tape to break even, and ordered few copies because popular titles had risk of going out of fashion. This caused chronic stockouts on new releases and frustrated customers. Under the revenue sharing contract, Blockbuster paid $8 per tape but split 45% of rental revenue with the studio. Break even at $3 * 0.55 = $1.65 net per rental was reduced to about 5 rentals. Blockbuster ordered 4-5x more copies per title, increased new release availability from about 40% to 80%, captured market share from mom-and-pop stores, and both Blockbuster and the studios saw profits rise. Compute the effect using a newsvendor model with stochastic demand. Then discuss why the coordination collapsed once Netflix entered and streaming arrived: the contract solved the rental market problem beautifully but could not address a technological disruption. Close with a supplier scorecard exercise for a Toyota tier-one supplier of exterior body panels, showing how Toyota weights quality (40%), delivery (25%), cost (20%), technology (10%), and responsiveness (5%).
setup_requirements: Newsvendor spreadsheet with contract comparison module
Case study: 'Blockbuster Entertainment' (HBS 9-601-050)
Sample supplier scorecards from Toyota, Honda, and Nissan
Liker and Choi (2004) HBR article on supplier partnerships

review.key: Wholesale Price Contracts Leave Money on the Table
review.value: A simple wholesale price contract cannot achieve chain-optimal quantities because it does not align retailer incentives with chain profit; more sophisticated contracts can recover the lost profit with proper design

review.key: Buyback and Revenue Sharing are Mathematically Equivalent
review.value: Under specific conditions, buyback contracts and revenue sharing contracts can produce identical quantities and profits; the choice between them is about administrative ease, accounting treatment, and risk perception

review.key: Contracts Solve Coordination but Not Disruption
review.value: Brilliant contracts can align incentives within a stable environment but cannot compensate for fundamental technological or market shifts; the Blockbuster revenue-sharing contract was optimal for its era but irrelevant when streaming changed the product

The most resilient supply chains are built on deep supplier relationships with shared goals and information, not on contractual levers alone; Toyota's lasting advantage versus Detroit came from how suppliers were treated, not from cleverer contract clauses

class_schedule.topic: The Bullwhip Effect and Information Sharing
learning_objectives: Explain the bullwhip effect and its four root causes: demand signal processing, order batching, price fluctuations, and rationing games
Quantify variance amplification through a multi-echelon chain using simple models
Identify coordination mechanisms that mitigate the bullwhip effect including information sharing and VMI
Analyze historical examples of bullwhip in consumer products, semiconductors, and the COVID toilet paper shortage
readings: Lee, Padmanabhan & Whang, 'The Bullwhip Effect in Supply Chains,' Sloan Management Review (Spring 1997)
Lee, Padmanabhan & Whang, 'Information Distortion in a Supply Chain: The Bullwhip Effect,' Management Science (1997)
Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 10
Sterman, 'Modeling Managerial Behavior: Misperceptions of Feedback in a Dynamic Decision Making Experiment,' Management Science (1989)

lecture_notes.introduction: In the 1990s, Procter & Gamble's logistics managers noticed a puzzle. Retail sales of Pampers diapers were remarkably stable week after week, but distributor orders to P&G were highly volatile, and P&G's orders to its own suppliers were more volatile still. Going upstream through the chain, variability amplified at every stage, creating massive swings in demand that had nothing to do with actual customer behavior. This phenomenon is the bullwhip effect, named by P&G and characterized formally by Hau Lee and colleagues at Stanford in their 1997 Sloan Management Review article. The bullwhip effect is not merely an academic curiosity; it costs the global supply chain tens of billions of dollars per year in excess inventory, capacity underutilization, unnecessary expediting, and stockouts. John Sterman's famous Beer Game simulation, played by MBA students and executives for decades, recreates the dynamic in 45 minutes and consistently produces wild oscillations even when end-customer demand is nearly constant. The COVID-19 pandemic displayed the bullwhip effect at planetary scale in 2020-2022, as a modest shift in consumer purchasing of toilet paper, household cleaning, and home office products triggered multi-quarter oscillations in raw materials, manufacturing, shipping, and retail inventory. The 2020-2023 automotive semiconductor crisis is another case study: a 10% dip in car sales in spring 2020 caused automakers to cancel chip orders, which were immediately absorbed by consumer electronics makers scaling up for work from home; when car demand rebounded a few months later, automakers found themselves at the back of the queue for chips. Ford alone estimated the semiconductor shortage cut 2021 production by 1.3 million vehicles and about $2 billion in profit. Understanding the four root causes of bullwhip, and the mechanisms that mitigate it, is essential for any supply chain manager who wants to avoid becoming another case study.
key_concepts: Bullwhip definition: variance of orders at upstream stages exceeds variance of customer demand; measured by the ratio Var(orders)/Var(demand), values above 1 indicate amplification
Cause 1 - Demand signal processing: each stage uses observed orders as a forecast, and when forecasts are based on recent orders each stage over-reacts to temporary spikes or dips
Cause 2 - Order batching: fixed ordering costs, full truckloads, or periodic review policies lead to lumpy orders upstream even when downstream demand is smooth

Cause 3 - Price fluctuations: trade promotions, forward buying, and volume discounts cause customers to buy ahead of need; orders become driven by price rather than consumption, creating artificial spikes

Cause 4 - Rationing and shortage gaming: when supply is short, suppliers allocate based on orders, so buyers place inflated orders hoping to get a larger allocation; when the shortage ends, everyone cancels simultaneously

Variance amplification math: for a simple AR(1) demand model with moving average forecast and lead time L, the order variance at the retailer is approximately Var(demand) * (1 + 2L/n + 2L^2/n^2) where n is the forecast window
Information sharing: giving upstream stages visibility into actual point-of-sale data removes the need to forecast from orders, cutting the demand signal cause of bullwhip
Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI): the supplier takes responsibility for managing the buyer's inventory; P&G manages Walmart's diaper inventory directly, eliminating several layers of order distortion

CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment): formal framework for buyer and supplier to jointly create a single forecast and replenishment plan; implemented at Walmart, Target, Lowe's

Everyday low pricing (EDLP): eliminates trade promotions and forward buying, removing the price-fluctuation cause of bullwhip; a key reason Walmart's supply chain is less volatile than trade-promotion-driven competitors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the Beer Game dynamics. Four stages (retailer, wholesaler, distributor, factory), each placing orders weekly with a 2-week lead time, seeing only its own order history. Customer demand is 4 cases/week for the first 4 weeks, then jumps permanently to 8 cases/week. Simulate the order patterns: retailer orders rise slowly, then overshoots as inventory drops and backlog builds; wholesaler sees the retailer's surge and orders much more aggressively; distributor sees the wholesaler's surge and panics; factory receives wildly oscillating orders and cannot keep up. Within 20 weeks, factory production peaks at 30-40 units/week to chase demand that never exceeded 8, then drops to near zero as excess inventory flows down. Compute variance at each stage and show amplification of 4-10x. Then apply the COVID toilet paper case: a 20% shift from office/industrial to home-use toilet paper, combined with panic buying, triggered a two-quarter bullwhip that saw Georgia-Pacific and Procter & Gamble running factories 24/7 while retailers still had empty shelves. Close with the semiconductor case: trace the 10% automotive demand drop in Q2 2020 through to the 1.3 million vehicle production cuts at Ford in 2021.
setup_requirements: Beer Game simulation software or board game kit
Excel or Python model for computing order variance through a multi-stage chain
Case studies on COVID toilet paper shortage and semiconductor crisis
Lee et al. (1997) Sloan Management Review article

review.value: The bullwhip effect is not a random outcome; it arises from specific structural causes that can be diagnosed and addressed. Blaming bullwhip on 'unpredictable demand' is wrong; the demand was predictable, the chain amplified it

review.key: Information Sharing is the First Fix
review.value: Giving upstream stages visibility into true demand eliminates the need to forecast from orders and is the single most effective bullwhip mitigation; Walmart's Retail Link system delivered this to 30,000 suppliers 30 years ago

review.key: Contract Terms Can Amplify or Dampen
review.value: Trade promotions, large minimum order quantities, and rationing policies all amplify bullwhip; EDLP, small order batches, and capacity reservation contracts all dampen it. Contract design matters as much as information

A chain in which each stage optimizes locally will always produce bullwhip; coordination mechanisms that force stages to consider chain-wide impact (VMI, CPFR, revenue sharing) deliver far better outcomes than cleverer local algorithms

class_schedule.topic: Supply Chain Risk Management and Resilience
learning_objectives: Identify and classify supply chain risks: operational, disruption, financial, and geopolitical
Apply risk assessment frameworks including probability-impact matrices and value-at-risk
Design resilient supply chains using redundancy, flexibility, and visibility
Analyze case studies of major disruptions: Thailand floods, Tohoku earthquake, Suez blockage, COVID-19
readings: Sheffi, The Power of Resilience: How the Best Companies Manage the Unexpected (2015), Ch. 1-5
Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 6
Simchi-Levi, 'Find the Weak Link in Your Supply Chain,' Harvard Business Review (June 2015)
Tang, 'Perspectives in Supply Chain Risk Management,' International Journal of Production Economics (2006)

lecture_notes.introduction: For decades, supply chain managers were rewarded for cutting inventory, consolidating suppliers, chasing the lowest cost country, and driving lean to the limit. These strategies looked brilliant until disaster hit. In March 2011, a 9.0 magnitude earthquake and tsunami struck Tohoku, Japan, destroying a Renesas semiconductor plant that made specialty microcontrollers used in roughly 40% of the world's cars; Toyota, Nissan, Honda, Ford, and GM all slashed production for months because there was no backup source. In October 2011, floods in Thailand inundated the industrial parks around Bangkok where Western Digital and Seagate manufactured 25% of the world's hard disk drives; global HDD prices doubled and stayed elevated for over a year. In March 2021, the Ever Given container ship wedged across the Suez Canal for six days, halting 12% of global trade and creating weeks of shipping knock-on effects. And of course COVID-19 from 2020 onward produced the largest and longest supply chain disruption in living memory, with cascading effects from PPE shortages to semiconductor famines to ocean freight rates rising 10x and then crashing. These events revealed that lean, low-redundancy supply chains were fragile in ways their designers had not imagined. Yossi Sheffi of MIT argued in The Power of Resilience that the best companies do not just manage risk once and check the box; they build organizational muscle for disruption through redundancy (strategic inventory, multi-sourcing), flexibility (interchangeable components, agile production, cross-trained workers), and visibility (control towers, supplier risk mapping). This session develops the conceptual framework for risk management and walks through case studies of firms that handled disruption well (Nokia, Cisco, Toyota in some events) and those that handled it poorly (Ericsson after a 2000 Philips fab fire, Ford during the 2021 chip crisis).
key_concepts: Risk classification: operational risks (quality defects, daily disruptions), disruption risks (natural disasters, strikes, plant fires), financial risks (currency, counterparty), geopolitical risks (tariffs, sanctions, war)
Probability-impact matrix: plot each identified risk by likelihood and business impact; focus attention on high-impact risks regardless of probability
Value-at-risk (VaR): expected financial loss at a given confidence level; extends financial risk measurement to supply chain events
Single points of failure: a supplier, facility, mode, or person whose disruption would halt the chain; the most dangerous risks because they are invisible until they fail
Redundancy: strategic inventory buffers, multi-sourcing, dual-site production, alternate transportation modes; increases cost in normal times but preserves operations in disruption
Flexibility: interchangeable components, modular designs, agile production, flexible contracts; provides response capacity without carrying permanent excess
Visibility: real-time information about inventory, shipments, and supplier status; control towers provide a single pane of glass for rapid response

Nokia versus Ericsson (2000 Philips fab fire): Nokia detected the fire early and locked up alternate capacity globally within days; Ericsson accepted supplier assurances that everything was fine and lost $400M in missed sales and ultimately exited the handset market

Toyota post-Tohoku playbook: redesigned Rescue System that tracks over 650,000 parts and can identify exposed components within days; invested in supplier diversification and inventory buffers for critical items
Resilience triangle: measures the area between normal operations and the disruption curve; smaller triangle means faster recovery and less loss
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the 2000 Philips Albuquerque fab fire and the Nokia-Ericsson divergence. A lightning strike on March 17, 2000 caused a fire in a clean room at Philips' Albuquerque semiconductor plant, damaging wafers and shutting down production of radio frequency chips used by both Nokia and Ericsson in their mobile phones. Philips initially told both customers the delay would be a week. Nokia's supply chain manager Tapio Markki noticed unusual delays in a specific chip SKU and escalated within 48 hours; by two weeks after the fire Nokia had sent engineers to Philips, locked up alternate capacity at Philips facilities in Europe and Asia, and redesigned the affected products to use alternate chips. Ericsson accepted the initial estimate, did not escalate, and by the time they realized the problem was a multi-month outage they had no alternate capacity available. Nokia's Q2 2000 handset market share rose while Ericsson's collapsed, contributing to Ericsson's 2001 decision to merge mobile phones with Sony. Compute the profit impact: Ericsson later attributed $400M in lost sales and $1.7B in one-year loss partly to this event; Nokia gained an estimated $2-3B in market share value. Then sketch a risk register for a current firm (Tesla battery supply, Apple TSMC dependence) and identify where redundancy or flexibility investment would reduce tail risk.
setup_requirements: Case studies on Nokia-Ericsson, Tohoku, Thailand floods, Suez blockage, and COVID disruption
Risk assessment spreadsheet templates from Sheffi
Supply chain risk registers from real firms (anonymized)
Video clips of Ever Given Suez blockage for discussion

review.key: Efficiency and Resilience are in Tension
review.value: Every inventory buffer, every redundant supplier, every alternate facility costs money in normal times; the right level of resilience is a business decision about tail risk tolerance, not an engineering optimum
The Nokia-Ericsson difference was not preparation, it was detection and response speed; firms that see problems earliest and act fastest capture the scarce response resources before competitors

Assuming independent risks leads to underestimating catastrophic scenarios; COVID simultaneously disrupted demand, supply, labor, and transportation, and none of the risk models built before 2020 had a multi-correlated scenario like that

No amount of technology or inventory substitutes for an organization that knows how to mobilize under disruption; Toyota and Nokia built the muscle over years, and you cannot grow it overnight when the earthquake hits

learning_objectives: Apply life cycle assessment (LCA) to evaluate environmental impact across supply chain stages
Design closed-loop supply chains with reverse logistics, remanufacturing, and recycling
Analyze the business case for sustainability including brand value, regulatory compliance, and cost reduction
Evaluate the circular economy framework and its implications for product and supply chain design
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 17
Kleindorfer, Singhal & Van Wassenhove, 'Sustainable Operations Management,' Production and Operations Management (2005)
Ellen MacArthur Foundation, 'Towards a Circular Economy' (2013)
Atasu, Guide & Van Wassenhove, 'Product Reuse Economics in Closed-Loop Supply Chain Research,' POM (2008)

lecture_notes.introduction: Supply chains are the primary mechanism by which the world's economic activity translates into environmental impact. According to CDP research, a typical consumer goods company's Scope 3 supply chain emissions are 5-10x larger than its direct operations emissions, meaning that Unilever's suppliers and logistics are more important for its carbon footprint than Unilever's own factories. Consumers, regulators, investors, and employees now demand that companies take responsibility for their full supply chain impact, not just what happens inside their own four walls. The EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive, the SEC climate disclosure rules, California's SB 253, and similar regulations worldwide are making Scope 3 reporting legally mandatory. Major firms have set aggressive commitments: Walmart's Project Gigaton to remove a billion tons of CO2 from its supply chain by 2030, Apple's 2030 carbon neutrality commitment covering the full device life cycle, and Unilever's Climate Transition Action Plan. Beyond carbon, supply chains drive water use, deforestation, plastic pollution, and labor conditions. Sustainable supply chain management is about quantifying these impacts and redesigning the chain to reduce them at acceptable cost, often discovering that sustainability and profitability align (energy efficiency, packaging reduction, reverse logistics for valuable materials) while sometimes requiring real trade-offs. The circular economy framework, popularized by the Ellen MacArthur Foundation, proposes that waste should be designed out, products should be kept in use as long as possible through repair and remanufacturing, and materials should flow back into the economy at end of life. Companies such as Interface (carpet), Caterpillar Reman, Patagonia Worn Wear, and Renault's Choisy-le-Roi remanufacturing plant demonstrate that circular business models can be profitable and scalable. This session develops LCA, reverse logistics design, and the economic framework for closed-loop supply chains.
key_concepts: Life cycle assessment (LCA): ISO 14040/14044 methodology for quantifying environmental impact from raw material extraction through manufacturing, distribution, use, and end-of-life; cradle-to-gate or cradle-to-grave scope

Scope 1, 2, 3 emissions: Scope 1 is direct emissions from owned sources, Scope 2 is purchased electricity, Scope 3 is everything else including supply chain and product use; for most brands Scope 3 dominates
Reverse logistics: flows of used products from customers back to collection, sorting, refurbishment, remanufacturing, or disposal; organized very differently from forward logistics

Remanufacturing: restoring used products to like-new condition through disassembly, cleaning, repair, and reassembly; Caterpillar Reman restores engine components to new-part warranty for 50-60% of new cost
