Closed-loop supply chain: forward and reverse flows integrated into a single chain; enables value recovery and reduces raw material demand
Circular economy principles: design out waste and pollution, keep products and materials in use, regenerate natural systems; Ellen MacArthur Foundation framework adopted by over 1000 organizations

Cradle-to-cradle design (McDonough & Braungart): products designed so that all materials are either biological nutrients that safely return to nature or technical nutrients that cycle through industrial loops indefinitely

Extended producer responsibility (EPR): regulations requiring manufacturers to take back products at end of life; EU WEEE Directive for electronics, packaging EPR laws in most of Europe, and emerging US state laws

Interface Mission Zero: carpet tile company committed to zero environmental footprint by 2020, achieved through modular design, take-back programs, recycled content, and biological fiber; case study in business-case sustainability

Sustainability paradox: what is good for the environment is often cheaper over time (energy efficiency, reduced waste) but requires up-front investment and organizational change that short-term pressures resist
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute an LCA comparison for packaging choices on a Unilever shampoo bottle. Option A: virgin PET plastic bottle, 25g per bottle, carbon intensity 2.5 kg CO2e/kg = 62.5 g CO2e per bottle. Option B: 100% recycled PET (rPET), 25g, carbon intensity 1.3 kg CO2e/kg = 32.5 g CO2e per bottle, savings of 30 g CO2e per bottle. At 500 million bottles per year, switching to rPET saves 15,000 tons CO2e per year, equivalent to taking about 3,200 cars off the road. Then add cost: virgin PET at $1.20/kg versus rPET at $1.50/kg (a 25% premium, but falling as recycling infrastructure matures). Annual cost impact = 500M * 0.025 kg * $0.30 = $3.75M. Compare to the value of carbon reduction at $50/ton internal carbon price = $750K, so the direct economic case falls short but the brand value and regulatory insurance may easily close the gap. Then design a reverse logistics network for Patagonia's Worn Wear program: drop-off bins at stores, consolidation to regional sorting centers, triage into repair, resale, or recycling; compute the economics versus disposing and selling only new product. Close with Caterpillar Reman: a component returned to Cat for credit, disassembled, restored, and resold at 50-60% of new price still delivers margins that exceed new production because raw material costs are avoided.
setup_requirements: LCA software or simplified spreadsheet with carbon intensity factors
Case studies: Interface Mission Zero, Patagonia Worn Wear, Caterpillar Reman
CDP supply chain report and Walmart Project Gigaton data
Ellen MacArthur Foundation circular economy framework materials

review.key: Scope 3 is Where the Impact Lives
review.value: Reducing Scope 1 and 2 emissions without addressing Scope 3 leaves 80-90% of the carbon footprint untouched; real sustainability programs start with supply chain mapping and supplier engagement

Used products still contain valuable materials, components, or functional assemblies; reverse logistics that captures this value turns waste streams into profit centers while cutting environmental impact

You cannot make a product circular after the fact; modularity, material choice, repairability, and disassembly must be designed in from the beginning. Fairphone and Framework Laptop are consumer electronics examples that prove this is possible

EU rules on CSRD, SEC climate disclosure, and similar laws worldwide are making supply chain sustainability a legal requirement, not a marketing option; firms that treat it as a compliance burden are behind, firms that treat it as a strategic lever are ahead

class_schedule.topic: Digital Supply Chain: IoT, Blockchain, and AI
learning_objectives: Evaluate the application of IoT sensors and digital twins in supply chain visibility and predictive maintenance
Analyze blockchain use cases for traceability, smart contracts, and supply chain finance
Apply machine learning methods to demand forecasting, anomaly detection, and inventory optimization
Assess the organizational and data requirements for successful supply chain digital transformation
readings: Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 18
Ivanov, Dolgui & Sokolov, 'The Impact of Digital Technology and Industry 4.0 on the Ripple Effect and Supply Chain Risk Analytics,' International Journal of Production Research (2019)
Gaur & Gaiha, 'Building a Transparent Supply Chain,' Harvard Business Review (May 2020)
McKinsey Global Institute, 'Digital Globalization: The New Era of Global Flows' (2016)

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital technology is reshaping how supply chains are designed, operated, and optimized at a pace faster than at any time since the introduction of containers in the 1960s. IoT sensors now cost a few dollars each, making it economical to track temperature, humidity, shock, and location on individual pallets, boxes, or even products. Maersk and IBM launched TradeLens in 2018 to digitize ocean shipping documentation on a shared blockchain; although TradeLens shut down in 2022, the underlying idea of industry-wide data sharing persists in follow-on initiatives. Machine learning is transforming demand forecasting, with firms like Walmart and Amazon running neural network models over thousands of features (weather, prices, events, social media) that outperform classical time series models by 15-30% on MAPE. Digital twins, virtual replicas of physical supply chains, let planners test scenarios before making real decisions; Unilever built a digital twin of its European supply chain that cut planning cycle time from days to hours. Robotic process automation handles routine procurement tasks, and generative AI is beginning to draft contracts, summarize supplier communications, and write code for integration. The question is not whether to invest in digital supply chain technology but where the highest-value applications are for a given business. This session develops a framework for evaluating digital investments, walks through the major technology categories, and examines cases of success and failure. The failures are instructive: Ocado's pioneering warehouse automation works, but its technology licensing deals with Kroger and others have hit delays and cost overruns. Target's ill-fated Canadian expansion failed partly because a new SAP supply chain system was rolled out with unreliable master data and forecasting bugs. Digital transformation fails when it is treated as a technology project rather than an operational and organizational change.
key_concepts: Internet of Things (IoT) in supply chain: low-cost sensors (GPS, RFID, temperature, vibration) attached to assets, inventory, and vehicles; enables real-time visibility and condition monitoring
Digital twin: virtual model of a physical supply chain updated with real-time data; enables scenario simulation, what-if analysis, and predictive maintenance

Blockchain for traceability: distributed immutable ledger recording product origin and chain of custody; used by Walmart for leafy greens (cut contamination tracing from days to seconds), Everledger for diamonds, Maersk TradeLens for shipping docs
Smart contracts: self-executing contracts that release payment when predefined conditions are met (shipment arrives, quality confirmed); reduce counterparty risk and paperwork
Supply chain finance via blockchain: tokenizing invoices to enable low-friction factoring and dynamic discounting; programs by HSBC, Standard Chartered, and we.trade

Machine learning for demand forecasting: gradient boosted trees (XGBoost), deep neural networks (LSTM, transformers), and probabilistic models (Amazon SageMaker DeepAR); consistently outperform classical methods on complex patterns
Reinforcement learning for inventory optimization: agents learn optimal stocking policies through simulated interaction with demand; used by Amazon for certain categories
Autonomous mobile robots (AMRs): Kiva-style robots now deployed at scale in Amazon, FedEx, and many 3PL warehouses; improve pick rates 2-4x and enable denser storage
Control tower: integrated visibility platform that combines data from ERP, TMS, WMS, supplier portals, and external sources into a single pane of glass for supply chain decision making

Data governance requirement: digital supply chain depends on master data (items, locations, suppliers, customers) being accurate, consistent, and maintained; most digital failures trace back to data quality problems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through Walmart's food traceability blockchain pilot with IBM Food Trust. Before blockchain, tracing a bag of mangoes from a US store back to its farm of origin took Walmart team members an average of 6 days and 18 hours of phone calls and paperwork. With the blockchain pilot, participants scanned QR codes at each step of the chain (farm, packing house, distributor, DC, store), and tracing time dropped to 2.2 seconds. The impact on food safety is dramatic: in an E. coli outbreak, the difference between 6 days and 2 seconds determines whether millions of pounds of product are pulled (the safe bet with long trace times) or only the specific contaminated lots (the precise response enabled by blockchain). Walmart has since mandated blockchain traceability for leafy greens suppliers. Then show an ML forecasting case: Amazon Forecast improved SKU-level forecast accuracy by 15-20% over legacy methods for certain product categories, translating into 10-15% inventory reduction at maintained service levels. Close with a digital twin example: a Unilever European supply chain twin that ingests daily data from factories, DCs, and retailers and allows planners to simulate responses to disruptions (port closures, demand spikes) before committing decisions. Discuss failure modes: Target Canada's SAP rollout failed because item master data from Target US was not adapted for the Canadian market, producing cascading forecast and replenishment errors that emptied shelves during the launch.
setup_requirements: Demo access to a supply chain visibility platform (Project44, FourKites, or IBM Supply Chain Insights)
Machine learning demand forecasting notebook in Python (Prophet, DeepAR, XGBoost)
Case studies: Walmart IBM Food Trust, Target Canada SAP failure, Maersk TradeLens
Gartner Magic Quadrant for supply chain planning software

review.value: No analytics, AI, or blockchain solution works without clean master data; firms that skip data governance and try to layer advanced technology on top always fail. Invest in data discipline before buying tools

Real-time visibility into inventory, shipments, and orders is the prerequisite for everything else in digital supply chain; without it, AI and automation cannot make good decisions because they cannot see what is really happening

Blockchain value comes from cross-organization data sharing, which requires trust and agreement among parties that are often competitors; Walmart's success in food traceability came from its size and willingness to mandate participation, not from the technology alone

Machine learning models capture complex patterns but struggle with regime changes and structural breaks; human planners must review forecasts, especially during disruption, and know when to override the model

class_schedule.topic: Global Supply Chains and Geopolitical Risk
learning_objectives: Analyze the total landed cost of international sourcing including duties, tariffs, logistics, and inventory
Evaluate the impact of tariffs, trade agreements, and sanctions on sourcing decisions
Apply frameworks for geopolitical risk assessment including country risk ratings and concentration analysis
Assess the drivers and strategies of the nearshoring and friend-shoring trends since 2018
readings: Mangan, Lalwani & Butcher, Global Logistics and Supply Chain Management (4th ed.), Ch. 3-4
Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 6
Farrell, 'Weaponized Interdependence: How Global Economic Networks Shape State Coercion,' International Security (2019)
Baldwin, The Great Convergence: Information Technology and the New Globalization (2016)

lecture_notes.introduction: The era of frictionless globalization that began in the 1990s ran on three assumptions: that political relations between major economies would remain stable, that shipping costs would stay low and reliable, and that comparative advantage would keep rewarding production wherever costs were lowest. All three assumptions have been severely challenged since about 2018. The US-China trade war starting in 2018 imposed tariffs of 10-25% on hundreds of billions of dollars of goods, forcing Apple, Dell, HP, and thousands of smaller firms to reconsider where they manufactured. The COVID-19 pandemic and the 2021-2022 ocean freight crisis revealed that ocean shipping was neither cheap nor reliable. Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, Western sanctions on Russia, and tensions over Taiwan have made political risk a first-class consideration in supply chain design. The result is the largest supply chain reconfiguration since the 1990s: Apple shifting iPhone production from China to India and Vietnam (from under 1% to over 10% of iPhones assembled outside China in 5 years), Mexican manufacturing exports to the US hitting record highs, TSMC building fabs in Arizona and Japan, Intel investing $20 billion in Ohio, and Samsung building a $17 billion fab in Texas. The CHIPS and Science Act (2022) provides $52 billion in US subsidies to reshore semiconductor production, and the Inflation Reduction Act creates further incentives for domestic battery and clean energy supply chains. But relocating supply chains is slow and expensive, and comparative advantage has not gone away. Apple will not replace China overnight, because the ecosystem of component suppliers, skilled labor, and logistics in Shenzhen cannot be replicated quickly anywhere else. This session examines the framework for evaluating global sourcing, the real economics of tariffs and trade agreements, and the strategic frameworks for managing geopolitical risk.
key_concepts: Total landed cost: the full cost of getting a product from supplier to destination, including unit price, inbound freight, duties, tariffs, handling, insurance, and in-transit inventory carrying cost

Tariffs and trade barriers: ad valorem duties (percentage of value), specific duties (dollars per unit), anti-dumping duties, countervailing duties; average global applied tariff around 5% but peaks much higher on targeted goods

Harmonized tariff schedule: international classification system with over 5,000 product categories; correct classification determines the applicable duty rate and is a major source of customs disputes
Free trade agreements: USMCA, CPTPP, EU Single Market, ASEAN; reduce or eliminate tariffs among members but add rules-of-origin requirements
Rules of origin: specify the minimum regional value content or substantial transformation required for a good to qualify for FTA benefits; USMCA auto rules require 75% North American content
Sanctions and export controls: US export administration regulations, EU dual-use controls, Russia sanctions post-2022, Iran sanctions; restrict who can sell what to whom
Country risk: political stability, regulatory predictability, rule of law, currency convertibility; rated by agencies like the Economist Intelligence Unit and OECD
Concentration risk: when too much of the supply chain depends on a single country or region; TSMC in Taiwan (making ~90% of the world's advanced logic chips) is the most discussed example
Nearshoring: moving production closer to end markets to reduce transit times and geopolitical exposure; Mexico for US consumption, Eastern Europe and Morocco for EU consumption
Friend-shoring: concentrating supply chains in allied countries to reduce geopolitical risk; articulated by US Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen in 2022 as formal policy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute total landed cost for sourcing a $50 bicycle component from China versus Mexico for delivery to a US assembly plant in Ohio. China: unit price $32, ocean freight $2.50, US duty 25% under Section 301 = $8, insurance and brokerage $0.50, in-transit inventory carrying 30 days at 25% = 30/365 * $43 * 0.25 = $0.88, total landed = $43.88. Mexico: unit price $37, truck freight $2.00, duty 0% under USMCA, insurance $0.30, in-transit inventory carrying 4 days = 4/365 * $39.30 * 0.25 = $0.11, total landed = $39.41. Mexico wins by $4.47 per unit, even though the ex-works price is 16% higher. Annual volume 500,000 units = $2.23M savings, plus faster response time and lower tariff risk. Now add a scenario: if Section 301 tariffs were removed, China's total landed = $35.88, flipping the decision back to China by $3.53 per unit. This shows why firms watch trade policy so closely and why the current trajectory of maintained or increased tariffs is accelerating nearshoring. Then analyze Apple's China-to-India shift: iPhone 14 production in India started at less than 5% of units, reached 14% by 2024, and is targeting 25% by 2025. Discuss the operational hurdles: labor skill gaps, component supplier availability, logistics infrastructure. Close with a TSMC concentration analysis: what happens to Apple, AMD, Nvidia, Qualcomm, and the entire electronics industry if a Taiwan crisis disrupts TSMC even temporarily, and what are the realistic mitigation strategies given TSMC's multi-year production lead and unmatched process technology.
setup_requirements: US harmonized tariff schedule access and Section 301 tariff list
Total landed cost spreadsheet with duty, freight, and inventory components
Case studies: Apple's India expansion, Intel Ohio fab, TSMC Arizona, nearshoring to Mexico
Recent trade agreement summaries (USMCA, CPTPP, CHIPS Act)

review.key: Tariffs Are Just One Cost Line
review.value: A 25% tariff looks devastating but is often offset by lower freight, shorter lead times, lower inventory, and reduced risk when sourcing closer to market; always compute total landed cost including all elements

The share of a critical component from a single country or supplier is a measurable risk that can be addressed with dual sourcing, strategic inventory, or investment in alternate capacity; firms that ignore it are gambling on political stability

Rebuilding a manufacturing ecosystem takes 5-10 years, not 1-2; TSMC's Arizona fab, announced in 2020, is still ramping in 2024-2025 and costs more than its Taiwanese counterparts. Patience and subsidies are required

The shift from lowest-cost-country sourcing to allied-country sourcing is reshaping global trade flows in ways that will persist for decades; supply chain professionals must now think about geopolitics as a first-order design input

class_schedule.topic: Performance Measurement, SCOR Model, and Course Synthesis
learning_objectives: Apply the SCOR model (Plan, Source, Make, Deliver, Return, Enable) to map and analyze a supply chain
Select and interpret supply chain KPIs including perfect order rate, cash-to-cash cycle, and total supply chain cost
Integrate concepts from all 15 sessions into a coherent supply chain strategy framework
Evaluate trade-offs across cost, service, responsiveness, resilience, and sustainability objectives
readings: APICS/ASCM, Supply Chain Operations Reference (SCOR) Model, Version 12
Chopra & Meindl, Supply Chain Management (7th ed.), Ch. 3, 17-18
Lambert & Cooper, 'Issues in Supply Chain Management,' Industrial Marketing Management (2000)
Gartner Top 25 Supply Chains annual rankings and methodology

lecture_notes.introduction: What gets measured gets managed, and supply chains are no exception. The question is which metrics matter and how they should be combined into a coherent view of performance. The SCOR model (Supply Chain Operations Reference), developed by APICS (now ASCM) over 25 years, is the industry standard framework for describing, measuring, and improving supply chains. It decomposes any supply chain into six process categories - Plan, Source, Make, Deliver, Return, Enable - and defines hundreds of standardized metrics so that firms can benchmark against each other meaningfully. The top-level SCOR metrics include perfect order rate (did the right product arrive at the right place, right time, in the right quantity, in the right condition, with the right documentation), cash-to-cash cycle time (days from paying suppliers to collecting from customers), total supply chain management cost as a percent of revenue, and upside supply chain flexibility (time to respond to a 20% unplanned demand increase). Amazon's cash-to-cash cycle is famously negative (they collect from customers before they pay suppliers), turning working capital into a source of financing rather than a cost; this is worth tens of billions of dollars in free capital annually. Walmart's total supply chain cost as a percentage of revenue is among the lowest in mass retail, a structural advantage that feeds its everyday low price promise. Apple ranks #1 in the Gartner Top 25 Supply Chains year after year, not because any single metric is best but because the integrated combination of design, sourcing, operations, and customer experience is unmatched. This final session pulls together the whole course: the strategy framework from Session 1, the quantitative inventory and network models, the supplier and risk frameworks, and the digital and sustainability considerations. The objective is for students to leave with an integrated mental model for diagnosing any real supply chain and designing improvements. The final assignment is an integrated case analysis applying the full toolkit to a contemporary supply chain challenge of the student's choice.
key_concepts: SCOR top-level processes: Plan (balance supply and demand), Source (procure), Make (produce), Deliver (distribute), Return (reverse flows), Enable (infrastructure and governance)
SCOR metrics hierarchy: Level 1 strategic metrics, Level 2 configuration diagnostics, Level 3 process element details; enable benchmarking at different levels of granularity
Perfect order rate: the fraction of orders delivered complete, on time, damage free, and with correct documentation; a composite metric that captures service level holistically
Cash-to-cash cycle time: days of inventory + days of receivables - days of payables; measures working capital efficiency; negative cycles (Dell and Amazon) are the hallmark of supply chain excellence
Total supply chain cost: sum of planning, sourcing, manufacturing, warehousing, transportation, and returns costs as a percent of revenue; Gartner's Top 25 ranking uses variants of this metric
Upside flexibility: days to respond to a 20% unplanned demand increase without cost penalty; captures the responsiveness dimension
Inventory days of supply: average inventory divided by daily cost of goods sold; 60-90 days typical for industrial, 30-45 for grocery, 20-30 for high-turn retail

Gartner Top 25 methodology: weighted combination of peer opinion, financial metrics (inventory turns, revenue growth, ROA), and corporate social responsibility scores; Apple, Unilever, P&G, Schneider Electric, Cisco dominate the rankings
Balanced scorecard for supply chain: financial, customer, internal process, and learning dimensions aligned to strategy; prevents over-optimization on one dimension at the expense of others
Continuous improvement cycle: measure, benchmark, identify gaps, design interventions, implement, measure again; the foundation of long-term supply chain excellence
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative SCOR benchmark of three retail supply chains: Walmart, Amazon, and Target. Walmart: inventory days 46, cash-to-cash -5 days (negative), total supply chain cost 4.5% of revenue, perfect order 95%+. Amazon: inventory days 42, cash-to-cash -30 days (strongly negative, funded by Prime prepayments), total supply chain cost 12% of revenue (higher due to e-commerce economics), perfect order 98%+. Target: inventory days 55, cash-to-cash +5 days, total supply chain cost 6% of revenue, perfect order 92%. Discuss what each profile reveals: Walmart is the cost leader with tight working capital; Amazon accepts higher cost to deliver premium service and uses prepayment to fund working capital; Target is competitive but not leading on any dimension, consistent with its 'cheap chic' positioning. Then apply SCOR to a student case: map a specific company's Plan-Source-Make-Deliver-Return processes, identify the two or three processes with the largest performance gaps versus best-in-class, and design a 12-month improvement plan. Close with a synthesis exercise: given a new firm with a chosen competitive strategy, walk through the full supply chain design choices from Session 1 through Session 14 and produce a coherent integrated design. This is the structure of the final examination.
setup_requirements: SCOR model reference document and metric definitions
Gartner Top 25 current year rankings and historical data
Financial data for comparison (inventory, receivables, payables) from company 10-Ks
Final project rubric and case assignment instructions

review.value: Choose metrics carefully because the organization will optimize for exactly what you measure; a warehouse judged on throughput alone will ignore damage rates, and a forecaster judged on bias will accept wild variance to stay unbiased

review.key: Cash-to-Cash is the Ultimate Integration Metric
review.value: It simultaneously captures inventory efficiency, customer collections, and supplier payment terms; improving it requires coordination across operations, sales, and finance, making it an excellent integration KPI for the whole firm

The value of a standard model is that performance can be compared across firms and industries using the same definitions; without SCOR, most 'benchmarks' are apples-to-oranges comparisons that tell you nothing actionable

The course comes back to its starting point: the supply chain is not a back-office function, it is the operational expression of the firm's strategy, and designing it well is one of the most consequential things a general manager ever does

quizzes.title: Quiz 1: Strategy, Forecasting, and Aggregate Planning

questions.prompt: A fast-fashion retailer is choosing between two supply chain designs for a new denim line. Design A: Chinese factory, 90-day lead time, unit cost $12, minimum order 5000 units per SKU. Design B: Mexican factory, 20-day lead time, unit cost $17, minimum order 500 units per SKU. Expected selling price is $45. Demand for each of 20 SKUs is forecast at mean 3000 with standard deviation 1500 over the 10-week selling season. Unsold units must be marked down to $15. Use the newsvendor framework and Fisher's efficient vs responsive framework to recommend one design. Show all calculations including the critical ratio, the optimal order quantity for each, expected profit per SKU, and the strategic fit reasoning.
questions.worked_answer: Critical ratio Cu/(Cu+Co): Cu = 45-12 = 33 (Design A) or 45-17 = 28 (Design B), Co = 12-15 = -3 for A (salvage exceeds cost, meaning Co = 12-15... actually since salvage $15 > cost A $12, the overage cost is negative, meaning no real penalty for over-ordering A; more realistically the markdown price $15 is below unit cost B so Co_B = 17-15 = 2. For Design A assume minimum salvage of cost so Co_A = 0 effectively, giving ratio ~1 which means order the max allowed or the MOQ. For Design B, ratio = 28/(28+2) = 0.933, z ~ 1.50, Q* = 3000 + 1.50*1500 = 5250 per SKU. Design A expected profit per SKU at MOQ=5000: revenue at mean demand 3000 * 45 = 135000, cost 5000*12 = 60000, salvage 2000*15 = 30000, profit = 105000. Design B at Q*=5250: revenue min(5250,3000)*45 avg 3000*45 = 135000, cost 5250*17 = 89250, salvage 2250*15 = 33750, profit = 79500 at mean demand. Design A looks more profitable at mean demand but the real comparison requires full expected profit over the demand distribution including stockout penalties on Design B's smaller orders. Strategic fit: fashion denim is an innovative product (short cycle, uncertain demand, high implied uncertainty); Fisher's framework recommends a responsive chain. Design B's 20-day lead time enables mid-season replenishment that Design A cannot, converting the forecast from a 10-week bet into a 2-week bet where errors can be corrected. The MOQ of 500 enables postponement and risk pooling across SKUs. Recommended: Design B, because the strategic fit value (reduced markdowns, chasing winners, killing losers) exceeds the $5/unit cost penalty. Zara's entire business model is built on this logic.
questions.rubric: Critical ratio calculation (3 points), Q* computation for both options (3 points), expected profit per SKU for each (4 points), Fisher framework identification as innovative (3 points), strategic fit reasoning with responsive chain recommendation (4 points), reference to Zara or similar real example (3 points). Total 20 points. Deduct for using only point-forecast comparison without addressing demand variability.

questions.prompt: A regional hospital is forecasting monthly demand for surgical masks using the last 12 months of consumption: Jan 8000, Feb 9200, Mar 10500, Apr 11800, May 13400, Jun 14900, Jul 16600, Aug 18300, Sep 20000, Oct 21500, Nov 23200, Dec 24900. Compute (a) a 3-period moving average forecast for January of next year, (b) simple exponential smoothing with alpha=0.3 starting from F_Jan = 8000, and (c) explain why both methods will perform badly and recommend a better method with justification. Compute MAD for the simple exponential smoothing method over months 2-12.
questions.worked_answer: (a) 3-period moving average for January next year = (21500 + 23200 + 24900)/3 = 23200 units. (b) Exponential smoothing with alpha=0.3: F_Feb = 0.3*8000 + 0.7*8000 = 8000 (since F_Jan=actual). F_Mar = 0.3*9200 + 0.7*8000 = 8360. F_Apr = 0.3*10500 + 0.7*8360 = 9002. Continuing: F_May ~ 9842, F_Jun ~ 10911, F_Jul ~ 12108, F_Aug ~ 13455, F_Sep ~ 14911, F_Oct ~ 16438, F_Nov ~ 17957, F_Dec ~ 19530. F_Jan_next = 0.3*24900 + 0.7*19530 = 21141. Both forecasts lag the persistent upward trend: the moving average predicts 23200 when the actual trend points to ~26600, and exponential smoothing predicts 21141, even further off. MAD for exponential smoothing over months 2-12: errors are Feb=1200, Mar=2140, Apr=2798, May=3558, Jun=3989, Jul=4492, Aug=4845, Sep=5089, Oct=5062, Nov=5243, Dec=5370. Sum = 43786, MAD = 43786/11 = 3981. (c) Both methods fail because the data has a strong linear trend and neither moving average nor simple exponential smoothing can track a trend. The correct method is Holt's double exponential smoothing which separately smooths level and trend. With alpha=0.3 and beta=0.2, Holt's would track the trend component and project F_Jan_next ~ 26500-27000, close to the linear extrapolation. This is exactly the COVID PPE forecasting problem: many hospitals using simple methods systematically under-forecast demand and ran out of masks in 2020.
questions.rubric: Moving average calculation (3 points), exponential smoothing series computation (5 points), MAD calculation (3 points), identification of trend as root cause (3 points), recommendation of Holt's method with justification (3 points), connection to real PPE shortage example (3 points). Total 20 points.

questions.prompt: A manufacturer has quarterly demand forecast Q1=2000, Q2=3500, Q3=4500, Q4=1000 units. Regular capacity is 2500/quarter at $50/unit, overtime up to 500/quarter at $75, subcontract up to 800 at $90, inventory holding $8/unit/quarter, starting inventory 0. Compute total cost for a pure level strategy (constant 2750/quarter) and a mixed strategy (regular at 2500, overtime as needed, minimal inventory). Determine which is cheaper and explain why.
questions.worked_answer: Level strategy at 2750/quarter: Q1 production 2750, demand 2000, end inventory 750; Q2 production 2750, start 750, demand 3500, end 0; Q3 production 2750, start 0, demand 4500, shortage of 1750 which must be covered somehow - this means level at 2750 cannot satisfy demand in Q3 alone. Need to pre-build. Try level at 2750 but start earlier: Q1 end inv 750, Q2 end inv 0, Q3 shortage 1750. Level strategy fails at this constant rate. Try level at 3250: Q1 prod 3250 (exceeds 2500 regular, needs 500 OT + 250 subcontract), Q2 prod 3250 (same), Q3 prod 3250 (same), Q4 prod 3250 (demand only 1000, end inv accumulates). Costs: Regular 2500*50=125000/q, OT 500*75=37500/q, sub 250*90=22500/q, total production cost 185000/q * 4 = 740000. Plus inventory: Q1 end inv 1250, Q2 end inv 1000, Q3 end inv -250 (shortage, skip for now), Q4 end inv 2000. Avg inv ~ 1250, holding cost = 1250*8*4 = 40000. Total level ~ 780000. Mixed strategy: Q1 produce 2000 regular (100000), Q2 produce 2500 reg + 500 OT + 500 subcontract = 2500*50+500*75+500*90 = 125000+37500+45000 = 207500 to build 0 extra, demand 3500 met exactly; need to build inventory earlier for Q3. Q1 produce 2500 reg + 500 OT = 162500, inv end 1000. Q2 prod 2500 reg + 500 OT = 162500, inv start 1000 + prod 3000 - demand 3500 = 500 end. Q3 prod 2500 reg + 500 OT + 800 sub = 125000+37500+72000 = 234500, inv 500+3800-4500 = -200 shortage. Need to push more to Q1/Q2. Q1 prod 2500+500=3000, Q2 prod 2500+500+800=3800, Q3 prod 2500+500+800=3800, Q4 prod 1000 reg=50000. Total supply 3000+3800+3800+1000=11600, demand 11000, surplus 600. Costs Q1 162500, Q2 234500, Q3 234500, Q4 50000. Inv: Q1 end 1000, Q2 end 1300, Q3 end 600, Q4 end 600. Avg ~875, holding=875*8*4=28000. Total mixed = 162500+234500+234500+50000+28000 = 709500. Mixed saves ~70500 over level. Mixed wins because it uses cheap regular capacity first, adds overtime only when needed, and uses expensive subcontracting only for peak quarters, while holding minimal inventory.
questions.rubric: Level strategy cost calculation with correct inventory accounting (7 points), mixed strategy cost calculation with feasible plan (7 points), identification that mixed is cheaper (2 points), explanation of why with reference to cost hierarchy (4 points). Total 20 points. Partial credit for feasible plans even if arithmetic differs slightly.

questions.prompt: An Amazon fulfillment center stocks a consumer electronics accessory with annual demand D = 50000 units, unit cost $18, ordering cost S = $250, and holding cost rate h = 30% per year. Daily demand is normal with mean 137 and standard deviation 45. Lead time from supplier is 7 days. Compute (a) EOQ, (b) total annual cost at EOQ including ordering and holding, (c) reorder point for 98% cycle service level, (d) annual inventory cost if service level is raised to 99.5%. Show all formulas.
questions.worked_answer: (a) H = 0.30 * 18 = $5.40 per unit per year. EOQ = sqrt(2*D*S/H) = sqrt(2*50000*250/5.40) = sqrt(4629630) = 2152 units. (b) Orders per year = 50000/2152 = 23.24. Ordering cost = 23.24 * 250 = $5809. Holding cost = (2152/2) * 5.40 = $5810. Total annual EOQ cost = $11619. The near-equality of ordering and holding cost at the optimum confirms EOQ minimum. (c) Lead-time demand = 137 * 7 = 959. Sigma of lead-time demand = 45 * sqrt(7) = 119. For 98% CSL, z = 2.05. Safety stock = 2.05 * 119 = 244 units. ROP = 959 + 244 = 1203 units. (d) At 99.5% CSL, z = 2.576. Safety stock = 2.576 * 119 = 307 units. Additional safety stock = 307 - 244 = 63 units. Additional annual holding cost = 63 * 5.40 = $340. Total safety stock holding cost at 99.5% = 307 * 5.40 = $1658, compared to 244 * 5.40 = $1318 at 98%. Total annual cost rises from $11619 + $1318 = $12937 to $11619 + $1658 = $13277, or 2.6% more cost for the extra 1.5% service level. Note the disproportionate cost as service level pushes toward 100%.
questions.rubric: EOQ calculation with correct formula and H computation (5 points), total annual cost verification (3 points), lead-time demand and sigma calculation (4 points), ROP for 98% (4 points), incremental cost analysis for 99.5% (4 points). Total 20 points.

questions.prompt: A specialty florist must decide how many roses to buy for Valentine's Day. Roses cost $1.20 per stem from the distributor, sell for $4.50 at peak, and unsold stems can be sold at $0.30 to a grocery chain on February 15. Historical demand is normally distributed with mean 1800 stems and standard deviation 400. Compute the optimal order quantity using the newsvendor framework and compute expected profit. Then show how the order would change if the florist could negotiate a return contract with the distributor at $0.80 per unused stem. Discuss the implications.
questions.worked_answer: Baseline: Cu = selling price - cost = 4.50 - 1.20 = $3.30 per stockout. Co = cost - salvage = 1.20 - 0.30 = $0.90 per leftover. Critical ratio = Cu/(Cu+Co) = 3.30/4.20 = 0.786. From standard normal table, z for 0.786 is approximately 0.795. Q* = mu + z*sigma = 1800 + 0.795*400 = 2118 stems. Expected profit requires the normal loss function: expected leftover L(z) at z=0.795 is sigma * L(0.795) where L(0.795) = 0.115 (from tables), so expected leftover = 400*0.115 = 46 stems. Expected sales = 1800 - 46 = 1754. Actually expected sales = mu - (Q - expected leftover)... more carefully: expected sales = min(D,Q) expected value = mu - expected shortage. Expected shortage at Q=2118 is sigma * L((Q-mu)/sigma) where L is the normal loss function at z=0.795. Approximating with normal loss: shortage unit-normal loss L(0.795) ~ 0.115, so expected shortage = 400*0.115 = 46 stems; wait that's leftover not shortage. Let me clarify: the standard loss function for upper tail shortage when Q > mu gives expected shortage. For z=0.795, L(z)=0.115 which represents expected shortage in units of sigma. Expected shortage = 400*0.115 = 46 stems. Expected sales = mu - shortage... no, expected sales = Q - expected leftover. Expected leftover = Q - mu + expected shortage = 2118 - 1800 + 46 = 364. Expected sales = 2118 - 364 = 1754. Expected profit = sales * margin - leftover * overage cost = 1754*3.30 - 364*0.90 = 5788.20 - 327.60 = $5460.60. With buyback at $0.80 per stem: Co = 1.20 - 0.80 = $0.40. Critical ratio = 3.30/(3.30+0.40) = 0.892. z for 0.892 is about 1.24. Q* = 1800 + 1.24*400 = 2296 stems. The buyback increases the optimal order by 178 stems. Expected profit with buyback (retailer side, assuming manufacturer accepts return) = 5788-ish with higher sales offsetting the different overage cost. The key insight: buyback contracts shift overstock risk upstream, letting the retailer order more and capturing more sales, while the manufacturer absorbs some risk in exchange for a higher wholesale price (not modeled here but implicit). This is why book publishers and DVD studios use buyback contracts.
questions.rubric: Critical ratio calculation (3 points), optimal Q* calculation (4 points), expected profit calculation using loss function (5 points), buyback Q* recalculation (4 points), interpretation and connection to real contract examples (4 points). Total 20 points. Allow for small arithmetic variations in loss function lookups.

questions.prompt: A national retailer has 16 regional warehouses, each independently managing safety stock for an SKU with daily demand mean 50, daily demand std dev 15, lead time 10 days, and 95% service level. Compute the total safety stock across the network. Then compute the total safety stock if all 16 warehouses are consolidated into a single national DC (pooling independent demand). Compute the percentage reduction. Finally explain what trade-offs the retailer must consider before centralizing and identify at least two real companies whose choices illustrate these trade-offs.
questions.worked_answer: Per warehouse: sigma_L = 15 * sqrt(10) = 47.43. For 95% CSL, z = 1.65. Safety stock per warehouse = 1.65 * 47.43 = 78 units. Total across 16 warehouses = 16 * 78 = 1248 units. Consolidated DC: daily demand mean = 16 * 50 = 800, daily sigma = sqrt(16) * 15 = 60 (standard deviation of sum of independent variables). sigma_L consolidated = 60 * sqrt(10) = 189.7. Consolidated safety stock = 1.65 * 189.7 = 313 units. Percentage reduction = (1248 - 313) / 1248 = 75%. This is the square root law: safety stock scales as sqrt(N) when N locations are pooled, so going from 16 to 1 cuts safety stock by a factor of 4 (from 16 * sqrt_per_location to 1 * sqrt_per_location scaled by sqrt(N)). Trade-offs to consider: (1) Outbound transportation cost rises because each customer is now served from a more distant facility, potentially adding many ton-miles. (2) Customer response time lengthens because shipments travel farther, hurting service commitments like next-day delivery. (3) Risk concentration: a disruption at the single DC now affects the entire network, whereas distributed stock has built-in redundancy. (4) Handling and capacity constraints at a single giant facility. Real examples: Amazon chose the opposite direction, adding more smaller fulfillment centers to reduce transit times even at the cost of higher safety stock, because next-day delivery was strategic. Netflix for physical DVDs (before streaming) centralized aggressively because mail delivery times were acceptable. Procter & Gamble periodically consolidates DCs when inventory costs dominate and distributes when response times matter, depending on the product category. The right answer is always cost-specific and service-specific, not universal.
questions.rubric: Per-warehouse safety stock (3 points), total decentralized SS (2 points), consolidated sigma calculation using sqrt(N) pooling (4 points), consolidated SS calculation (3 points), percentage reduction (2 points), identification of at least three trade-offs (3 points), at least two real examples with reasoning (3 points). Total 20 points.

quizzes.title: Quiz 3: Network Design, Transportation, and Sourcing

questions.prompt: A US firm ships electronics components from two origins to one destination. Option A: Shenzhen by ocean, 22-day transit, $3500 per 40ft container (ocean freight), $800 customs and brokerage, 25% Section 301 tariff on $200000 declared value. Option B: Guadalajara by truck, 4-day transit, $4200 per truckload (equivalent capacity), 0% tariff under USMCA, $200 customs and brokerage. Each container/truck holds 2000 units, each unit costs $100 before freight and duties. Annual holding cost rate is 25%. Compute total landed cost per unit for each option and recommend. Then analyze what happens if the tariff is removed.
questions.worked_answer: Option A (Shenzhen ocean): unit cost $100. Freight and brokerage per unit = (3500+800)/2000 = $2.15. Tariff = 25% of $100 = $25 per unit. In-transit inventory carrying: holding rate 25% applied to $127.15 average value (including freight and tariff) for 22/365 of a year = 0.25 * 127.15 * 22/365 = $1.92. Total landed A = 100 + 2.15 + 25 + 1.92 = $129.07 per unit. Option B (Guadalajara truck): unit cost $100. Freight and brokerage per unit = (4200+200)/2000 = $2.20. Tariff = $0. In-transit inventory = 0.25 * 102.20 * 4/365 = $0.28. Total landed B = 100 + 2.20 + 0 + 0.28 = $102.48 per unit. Recommendation: Option B wins by $26.59 per unit, driven almost entirely by the 25% tariff. At 100,000 units per year, savings = $2.66 million annually. Plus shorter lead time, lower risk, and less inventory exposure. If tariff is removed: Option A becomes 100 + 2.15 + 0 + 1.54 (recomputed holding without tariff in value) = $103.69. Option B remains $102.48. Now Mexico still wins but by only $1.21 per unit. The tariff is the swing factor. This is exactly why Apple has been shifting iPhone production to India and Vietnam since 2019 while maintaining a foothold in China for the non-tariffed markets.
questions.rubric: Option A total landed cost with all components (5 points), Option B total landed cost (4 points), recommendation with savings quantified (3 points), sensitivity to tariff removal (4 points), connection to real nearshoring example (4 points). Total 20 points. Small arithmetic variations acceptable.

questions.prompt: A Midwest retailer is deciding where to locate a new DC to serve four regional markets: Chicago (demand 400 tons/week, coordinates 88W 42N), Detroit (350, 83W 42N), Minneapolis (250, 93W 45N), St. Louis (300, 90W 39N). Use the center of gravity method to compute optimal coordinates. Then identify the nearest major city to those coordinates and discuss three real-world factors the center of gravity method ignores that would influence the final site selection.
questions.worked_answer: Total demand = 400+350+250+300 = 1300 tons. Weighted x = (400*88 + 350*83 + 250*93 + 300*90)/1300 = (35200+29050+23250+27000)/1300 = 114500/1300 = 88.08W. Weighted y = (400*42 + 350*42 + 250*45 + 300*39)/1300 = (16800+14700+11250+11700)/1300 = 54450/1300 = 41.88N. So optimal coordinates ~88.1W, 41.9N. This is near Rockford, Illinois, or the Chicago suburbs of Joliet/Aurora. Real-world factors center of gravity ignores: (1) Actual road network and shipping lanes: straight-line distance is replaced by actual highway miles, which can differ significantly around geographic barriers like the Great Lakes. (2) Labor market conditions: the optimal point may be in a rural area with thin labor availability, while a nearby city offers much better workforce access. Chicago and its suburbs have established logistics labor markets that Rockford or smaller towns do not. (3) Tax and economic development incentives: states compete aggressively for DC investments with property tax abatements, training subsidies, and infrastructure support; Indiana, Ohio, and Kentucky have each offered hundreds of millions to specific projects. Additional factors include interstate highway access, proximity to rail intermodal terminals, zoning and environmental constraints, and land cost. The correct approach is to use center of gravity as a starting point and then evaluate 5-10 candidate sites within 100 miles of that point using weighted factor rating or mixed integer programming with realistic constraints and cost components.
questions.rubric: Weighted x calculation (3 points), weighted y calculation (3 points), identification of nearest major city (2 points), three real-world factors with explanations (9 points, 3 each), recommended refinement approach (3 points). Total 20 points.

questions.prompt: A US auto OEM must decide how to source power electronics modules for its EV program. Annual volume 300000 units. Three suppliers are bidding. Supplier X (Mexican): unit price $1200, quality 100 ppm defects, 98% on-time delivery, strong lean production, English-speaking engineering team. Supplier Y (Chinese): unit price $980, quality 500 ppm defects, 92% on-time delivery, some IP protection concerns. Supplier Z (US insourced): unit price $1350, quality 80 ppm defects, 99% on-time delivery, full control but highest cost. Assume defect cost is $800 per defective unit (warranty, recall risk), lateness penalty is $0.50 per unit per late-delivery occurrence. Use the Kraljic matrix to classify this purchase, compute TCO for each supplier, and recommend. Discuss the strategic factors beyond pure TCO.
questions.worked_answer: Kraljic classification: power electronics modules are both high financial impact (300000 * ~$1000 = $300M annual spend) and high supply risk (specialized technology, limited qualified suppliers, critical to EV program), placing them firmly in the Strategic quadrant, which calls for partnership rather than pure cost competition. TCO computation: Supplier X: price $1200 + quality cost 100/1M * $800 = $0.08 + lateness cost 2% * $0.50 = $0.01 = $1200.09. Supplier Y: price $980 + 500/1M * $800 = $0.40 + 8% * $0.50 = $0.04 = $980.44. Supplier Z: price $1350 + 80/1M * $800 = $0.064 + 1% * $0.50 = $0.005 = $1350.07. Raw TCO ranking: Y cheapest, X middle, Z most expensive. But strategic factors beyond TCO: (1) IP protection: the EV program's competitive advantage depends on power electronics performance; Chinese IP concerns create existential risk. (2) Geopolitical and tariff exposure: future tariff increases on Chinese components could flip Y's cost advantage. (3) Quality risk in warranty and recall: 500 ppm sounds small but with 300K units that is 150 defective modules per year potentially triggering a safety recall costing $50M+. (4) Long-term capability development: strategic suppliers grow with the OEM; Chinese sourcing limits US workforce and capability development. (5) Supply chain resilience: Mexico is 3 days away; China is 22 days away with variable transit times. Recommendation: Supplier X (Mexico) for strategic reasons even though TCO is $220/unit higher than Y. The $66M annual cost difference buys insurance against IP loss, tariff risk, warranty disasters, and geopolitical disruption. Supplier Z would be even better for pure control but the $66M cost premium over X may not be justified without additional strategic value. Ford and GM both made similar decisions in 2022-2024 to bring EV battery and electronics supply closer to home despite higher unit costs.
questions.rubric: Kraljic classification with reasoning (4 points), TCO calculation for each supplier (6 points), recommendation with strategic reasoning (5 points), connection to real OEM decisions (3 points), discussion of at least three strategic factors beyond TCO (2 points). Total 20 points.

questions.prompt: A manufacturer sells through a retailer at wholesale $40/unit. Retailer sells to consumers at $80/unit. Production cost is $25/unit. Unsold units at retailer can be salvaged at $15. Demand is normal with mean 5000 and std dev 1000. Under a pure wholesale price contract, compute the retailer's optimal Q, the retailer's expected profit, the manufacturer's profit, and the total chain profit. Then compute the chain-optimal Q if both were integrated. Finally design a buyback contract at wholesale $40 and buyback price $28 and show that it achieves (approximately) chain-optimal quantity.
questions.worked_answer: Wholesale contract retailer view: Cu = 80-40 = $40 (margin on each sale), Co = 40-15 = $25 (cost of each leftover). Critical ratio = 40/65 = 0.615. z = 0.293. Q_retailer = 5000 + 0.293*1000 = 5293. Retailer expected sales approximately 5000-60 = 4940 (using normal loss function). Expected leftover = 5293-4940 = 353. Retailer profit = 4940*40 - 353*25 = 197600 - 8825 = 188775. Manufacturer revenue = 5293*40 - 5293*25 = 5293*15 = 79395 (margin on 5293 units sold to retailer). Total chain profit = 188775 + 79395 = 268170. Integrated view: Cu = 80-25 = $55, Co = 25-15 = $10. Critical ratio = 55/65 = 0.846. z = 1.02. Q_integrated = 5000+1020 = 6020. Chain profit at Q=6020 with loss function: expected sales ~ 4993, leftover ~ 1027. Profit = 4993*80 - 6020*25 - 1027*... wait chain view: revenue = expected sales * 80 + leftover * 15 = 4993*80 + 1027*15 = 399440 + 15405 = 414845. Total cost = 6020 * 25 = 150500. Chain profit = 414845 - 150500 = 264345. Hmm, should be higher than wholesale contract. Let me recompute wholesale: Retailer orders 5293, pays manufacturer 5293*40 = 211720. Retailer revenue = 4940*80 + 353*15 = 395200 + 5295 = 400495. Retailer profit = 400495 - 211720 = 188775 (matches). Manufacturer profit = 211720 - 5293*25 = 211720 - 132325 = 79395 (matches). Chain = 268170. Integrated chain: 6020*25 = 150500 cost. Revenue = 4993*80 + 1027*15 = 399440 + 15405 = 414845. Chain profit = 264345. Wait, this suggests the wholesale contract does better, which is wrong. Let me recheck integrated expected sales: for z=1.02, normal loss L(1.02) ~ 0.072, expected shortage = 1000*0.072 = 72. Expected sales = 5000 - 72 = 4928. Wait no, when Q > mu the expected sales = mu - shortage where shortage function for Q above mean is L(z)*sigma. Actually expected shortage = sigma * L(z) regardless, and expected sales = Q - expected leftover = Q - (Q-mu+expected shortage). Let me redo: expected leftover when Q=6020, z=1.02: E[leftover] = (Q-mu) + sigma*L(z) = 1020 + 72 = 1092. Expected sales = Q - leftover = 6020 - 1092 = 4928. Chain revenue = 4928*80 + 1092*15 = 394240 + 16380 = 410620. Chain profit = 410620 - 150500 = 260120. Still looks lower. I must be mis-applying loss function. Standard: E[sales] = mu - sigma*L(z) when computing for z = (Q-mu)/sigma positive. E[leftover] = Q - E[sales]. For Q=6020, z=1.02, L(1.02)=0.072. E[sales] = 5000 - 72 = 4928. Wait that's below 5000, but Q=6020 exceeds 5000 so sales should average near 5000. Actually E[sales] approaches mu asymptotically as Q grows; correct formula: E[sales at Q] = mu - expected_shortage_if_had_stocked_infinite_plus_leftover. Let me use: E[sales] = integral from 0 to Q of x*f(x)dx + Q*(1-F(Q)). For normal with mu=5000 sigma=1000, Q=6020: P(D<=Q)=F(1.02)=0.846. E[sales] ~ mu - sigma*phi(z) where phi is the loss function; more precisely E[sales] = mu * F(z) + sigma*phi(z)*(complicated) ... let me just use the result that for normal demand, E[min(D,Q)] = mu - sigma*L(z) where L(z)=phi(z)-z*(1-Phi(z)). At z=1.02, phi(1.02)=0.2396, 1-Phi(1.02)=0.1539, L(1.02) = 0.2396 - 1.02*0.1539 = 0.2396 - 0.1570 = 0.0826. E[sales] = 5000 - 1000*0.0826 = 4917. E[leftover] = 6020 - 4917 = 1103. Chain revenue = 4917*80 + 1103*15 = 393360 + 16545 = 409905. Chain profit = 409905 - 150500 = 259405. Still lower than wholesale 268170, suggesting my wholesale calculations are also off. Let me recompute wholesale retailer chain view properly: Q_retailer = 5293, z=0.293, L(0.293): phi(0.293)=0.3826, 1-Phi(0.293)=0.385, L = 0.3826 - 0.293*0.385 = 0.3826-0.1128 = 0.2698. E[sales] = 5000 - 269.8 = 4730. E[leftover] = 5293-4730=563. Retailer profit = 4730*80 - 563*... wait retailer paid 5293*40=211720 for the goods, gets 4730*80=378400 from sales + 563*15=8445 salvage = 386845 total, minus 211720 = 175125. Manufacturer profit = 211720 - 5293*25 = 79395. Chain = 254520. Now integrated chain profit 259405 > 254520, difference $4885, confirming integration is better. Good. Buyback contract at wholesale $40 and buyback $28: retailer's effective overage cost = 40-28 = $12 (lost cost when manufacturer refunds $28), Cu = 80-40 = $40. Critical ratio = 40/52 = 0.769, z = 0.736. Q_buyback = 5000+736 = 5736. Closer to chain-optimal 6020 but not exactly there; tune buyback price to exactly 29.23 to hit chain-optimal. The buyback contract shifts risk upstream and encourages the retailer to order more, approaching chain optimal. Both sides gain when the manufacturer sets wholesale and buyback prices to split the pie.
questions.rubric: Wholesale contract retailer Q and profit (5 points), manufacturer profit under wholesale (3 points), integrated chain-optimal Q (4 points), integrated chain profit (3 points), buyback contract design and analysis (5 points). Total 20 points. Given the complexity of normal loss function computations, accept answers within 5% for numerical values and full credit for correct methodology.

questions.prompt: Describe the four root causes of the bullwhip effect as identified by Lee, Padmanabhan, and Whang (1997) and match each to a specific observable business practice that aggravates it. Then identify the mitigation technique for each cause. Finally, explain how the 2020-2021 COVID toilet paper shortage illustrates at least three of the four causes simultaneously.
questions.worked_answer: Four root causes: (1) Demand signal processing: each stage uses recent orders as a forecast; a temporary spike causes upstream forecasts to jump, amplifying orders. Aggravating practice: forecasting from orders rather than POS data. Mitigation: share point-of-sale data upstream (Walmart Retail Link, VMI). (2) Order batching: fixed ordering costs, full truckloads, or periodic review lead to lumpy orders even with smooth demand. Aggravating practice: minimum order quantities, weekly or monthly ordering cycles. Mitigation: reduce ordering costs (e-procurement, EDI), smaller more frequent orders, consolidation of multiple products into one shipment. (3) Price fluctuations: trade promotions, forward buying, volume discounts cause orders to be driven by price not consumption. Aggravating practice: quarterly promotions, end-of-quarter discounts, forward-buy incentives. Mitigation: everyday low pricing (EDLP), activity-based pricing, eliminating promotion spikes. (4) Rationing and shortage gaming: when supply is short, allocation is based on orders, so buyers inflate orders hoping for bigger allocation. Aggravating practice: proportional allocation during shortage. Mitigation: allocate based on historical orders rather than current orders, strict MOQs, transparent capacity information. COVID toilet paper illustration: (A) Demand signal processing was massive: retailers saw 200-400% demand spikes in early 2020 from panic buying and treated them as permanent, placing large orders with manufacturers. (B) Price fluctuations were not a major factor since retailers kept prices roughly stable, so this cause was less active. (C) Order batching played a role as retailers moved to larger truckload orders to minimize transportation cost during the crisis. (D) Rationing and shortage gaming was enormous: as manufacturers rationed supply, retailers multiplied their orders hoping to get a larger share of scarce product; when supply normalized in mid-2020, those inflated orders came back as excess inventory and production cuts in late 2020 and 2021. The COVID toilet paper bullwhip is now a classic case of three-cause amplification, with Procter & Gamble and Georgia-Pacific running paper mills at full capacity for months then cutting back sharply as downstream inventory normalized.
questions.rubric: Four causes correctly identified (4 points), aggravating practice for each (4 points), mitigation technique for each (4 points), COVID toilet paper analysis with at least three causes mapped (8 points). Total 20 points.

questions.prompt: Compare Nokia's and Ericsson's responses to the March 2000 Philips Albuquerque fab fire. What specific decisions and organizational factors allowed Nokia to emerge stronger while Ericsson suffered lasting damage? Extract at least four lessons for supply chain risk management that apply to contemporary situations such as the semiconductor supply crisis or potential Taiwan disruption scenarios.
questions.worked_answer: The Philips Albuquerque fab made radio frequency chips used by both Nokia and Ericsson. A lightning-triggered fire damaged wafers and shut down one clean room. Philips initially told customers the delay would be one week. Nokia's response: within 48 hours Tapio Markki, a Nokia supply chain manager, noticed an anomaly in Philips shipment data, escalated internally, and within two weeks Nokia had sent engineers to Albuquerque, locked up alternate Philips capacity in Europe and Asia, and identified or requalified alternate chips for affected products. Nokia worked around the disruption with minimal customer impact. Ericsson's response: accepted Philips' initial one-week estimate, did not escalate, and did not start serious investigation until weeks later when it became clear the disruption was multi-month. By that time all alternate capacity had been secured by Nokia and other customers. Ericsson was unable to ship phones to customers for months and lost an estimated $400M in direct sales and $1.7B in total impact, contributing to Ericsson's 2001 decision to exit mobile phone manufacturing by merging with Sony. Lessons: (1) Detection speed is everything: Nokia saw the problem in days because line-level managers were watching operational data and empowered to escalate; Ericsson's hierarchy slowed detection. Contemporary application: invest in supply chain visibility platforms that show supplier operational status in real time, not just monthly reviews. (2) Response speed beats preparation: Nokia did not have a pre-built disaster plan for this exact fire, but it had the organizational reflexes to mobilize engineers, negotiate capacity, and redesign products within days. Build response capability, not just plans. (3) Scarce resources go to the fastest movers: during a disruption, alternate capacity, expedited freight, and engineering attention are finite. Firms that move first win them; firms that wait pay in lost sales. Contemporary application: during the 2020-2022 semiconductor shortage, Toyota's decision to stockpile chips after learning from the 2011 Tohoku earthquake gave it an 18-month advantage over Ford and GM. (4) Strategic redundancy is worth the cost: Nokia's multi-supplier strategy and broader qualified chip base gave it alternates; Ericsson's leaner supply base left it exposed. In contemporary context, TSMC concentration risk for advanced logic chips is the single largest identified supply chain risk, and firms that have not qualified alternates or built strategic inventory will be at the back of the queue if Taiwan is disrupted. (5) Bonus lesson: supply chain risk is a board-level issue, not just a purchasing department function; Ericsson's failure traced partly to organizational signals that discouraged escalation of supplier issues to senior management.
questions.rubric: Description of the Philips fire and its supply chain impact (3 points), Nokia's response actions (4 points), Ericsson's response failure (3 points), four lessons with contemporary application (10 points, 2.5 each). Total 20 points.

quizzes.title: Quiz 5: Sustainability, Digital, and Global Supply Chains

questions.prompt: A consumer goods firm is evaluating switching from virgin PET to 100% recycled PET for its beverage bottles. Annual volume 750 million bottles, current bottle weight 22g virgin PET at $1.25/kg with carbon intensity 2.5 kg CO2e per kg of material, alternative rPET at $1.65/kg with carbon intensity 1.1 kg CO2e/kg. Compute (a) the annual cost impact, (b) the annual carbon reduction in tons CO2e, (c) the implied internal carbon price at which the decision breaks even, (d) discuss at least three qualitative factors beyond direct cost and carbon that should influence the decision.
questions.worked_answer: (a) Material per bottle = 0.022 kg. Annual material = 750M * 0.022 = 16.5 million kg. Cost virgin = 16.5M * $1.25 = $20.625M. Cost rPET = 16.5M * $1.65 = $27.225M. Annual cost increase = $6.6M. (b) Carbon virgin = 16.5M * 2.5 = 41.25 million kg CO2e = 41,250 tons. Carbon rPET = 16.5M * 1.1 = 18.15 million kg = 18,150 tons. Annual carbon reduction = 23,100 tons CO2e. (c) Break-even internal carbon price = $6.6M / 23,100 tons = $286 per ton CO2e. This is higher than most corporate internal carbon prices (typically $50-100/ton) but lower than the EU ETS peak (~$100/ton in 2022-2023) and far below the social cost of carbon estimates of $190-400 per ton. (d) Qualitative factors: (i) Regulatory trajectory: EU requires 25% rPET in beverage bottles by 2025 and 30% by 2030 under the Single-Use Plastics Directive; California AB 793 has similar mandates. Acting now is largely compliance positioning rather than optional. (ii) Brand and consumer signaling: major brands including Coca-Cola, Pepsi, Nestle Waters, and Unilever have all made public rPET commitments; customers increasingly expect recycled content and firms that lag will lose shelf presence. (iii) Supply security for rPET: recycled plastic supply is growing but volatile; locking in long-term contracts with recyclers provides strategic security and may reduce the cost premium over time. (iv) Downstream recyclability benefits: using rPET validates the recycling ecosystem, increasing the value of collected PET and driving further investment. (v) Scope 3 reporting: under the EU CSRD and SEC proposed rules, firms must report and reduce scope 3 emissions; switching to rPET reduces reported scope 3 materially and supports science-based targets. The direct economic case alone may not close, but the combined case is usually positive.
questions.rubric: Annual cost impact (4 points), annual carbon reduction (4 points), implied carbon price (3 points), three qualitative factors with reasoning (9 points, 3 each). Total 20 points.

questions.prompt: A major retailer is considering implementing a blockchain-based product traceability system for its fresh produce category. Expected implementation cost $15M in year 1 with $3M annual operating cost. Current traceback time during a contamination event is 6 days; blockchain would reduce this to under 10 seconds. Historical data shows 2 contamination events per year, with average recall costs of $25M each including product destroyed, refunds, and reputation damage. Blockchain would reduce recall scope by approximately 70% due to precise traceback. Compute the NPV over 5 years at 10% discount rate. Then discuss whether the NPV captures the full value and identify at least three additional benefits or risks not reflected in the NPV.
questions.worked_answer: Annual benefit from reduced recall scope: 2 events * $25M * 70% reduction = $35M per year. Net annual benefit = $35M - $3M operating cost = $32M. Year 1 net = $32M - $15M implementation = $17M. Years 2-5 net = $32M each. Present values at 10%: Y1 = 17/1.1 = 15.45, Y2 = 32/1.21 = 26.45, Y3 = 32/1.331 = 24.04, Y4 = 32/1.464 = 21.86, Y5 = 32/1.611 = 19.87. NPV 5 years = 107.67M. Clearly positive and the payback is well within year 1. Additional benefits not captured in NPV: (i) Brand value protection: a well-handled contamination event builds trust, while a botched event can cost years of customer loyalty; this effect is hard to quantify but often exceeds direct recall costs. (ii) Regulatory positioning: FDA's Food Safety Modernization Act imposes increasing traceability requirements on US food supply chains; blockchain positions the retailer ahead of future mandates. (iii) Supplier quality signaling: requiring suppliers to participate in blockchain pressures them to improve their own food safety practices, cascading the benefits upstream. (iv) Cross-category expansion: the infrastructure built for produce can be reused for other categories (meat, seafood, pharmaceuticals), amortizing the investment over more products. Risks not captured: (i) Supplier adoption resistance: small farms may lack the technology or incentive to participate, creating gaps in the chain and requiring vendor development investment. (ii) Data quality: blockchain immutability amplifies bad data; if a supplier incorrectly records harvest information, the error is locked in permanently. (iii) Technology obsolescence: the current blockchain platform may be replaced by a different standard, requiring migration. Walmart actually implemented this system with IBM Food Trust starting in 2016 and mandated leafy greens suppliers to participate in 2019; the business case was driven by food safety and brand protection more than direct NPV.
questions.rubric: Annual benefit calculation (4 points), 5-year NPV with proper discounting (6 points), at least three additional factors/risks with discussion (6 points), connection to real Walmart implementation (4 points). Total 20 points. Allow small rounding variations in NPV.

questions.prompt: Synthesize the course: design an integrated supply chain strategy for a new premium US electric vehicle startup targeting the $60,000-80,000 market segment with planned production of 50,000 units per year by 2028. The strategy must address (a) supply chain type and strategic fit, (b) sourcing approach for critical components including batteries and semiconductors, (c) manufacturing and distribution footprint, (d) resilience and geopolitical risk management, (e) sustainability positioning, and (f) at least three key SCOR metrics you would track. Be specific and cite course concepts.
questions.worked_answer: Strategic fit: Premium EVs are innovative products with uncertain demand, short life cycles between model refreshes, and high margins; Fisher's framework recommends a responsive supply chain over an efficient one. Tesla's success with a responsive model and Rivian's and Lucid's struggles with ramp execution confirm that premium EVs need flexibility over cost minimization. (b) Sourcing: Batteries are Kraljic-strategic: high spend (roughly $10-15k per vehicle = $500-750M annual spend) and high supply risk. Recommend long-term partnership with LG Energy Solution, Samsung SDI, or Panasonic, ideally with some joint investment in US cell production under IRA incentives; secondary qualification of CATL if possible despite Chinese origin concerns. Semiconductors are Kraljic-strategic after the 2020-2023 crisis: dual-source for critical chips between TSMC (Taiwan and Arizona) and GlobalFoundries or Infineon; build strategic inventory of 60-90 days for highest-risk chips. Standard components (wiring harnesses, fasteners) are Kraljic-leverage with competitive bidding, but consolidated to a small number of tier-one suppliers for quality control. (c) Manufacturing footprint: Single US assembly plant in the Midwest or Southeast to qualify for USMCA and IRA benefits, reducing tariff and currency risk; Mexican manufacturing of selected subassemblies (wiring, interior) to capture lower labor cost while staying within USMCA origin requirements. No Chinese manufacturing for final vehicles given Section 301 tariffs and geopolitical concerns. Distribution through direct-to-customer order model following Tesla playbook, with regional delivery centers rather than traditional dealer networks. (d) Resilience and geopolitics: Dual-source critical components, maintain 60-90 day safety stock for strategic items, map tier-two and tier-three supply to identify Taiwan and China concentration, participate in CHIPS Act programs to accelerate domestic semiconductor supply. Build a control tower for real-time supplier visibility. Insurance and financial hedges for foreign exchange and raw materials. (e) Sustainability: Premium buyers care about the full lifecycle carbon footprint, making sustainability an order-winner rather than just compliance. Commit to carbon-neutral manufacturing using renewable electricity, recycled content aluminum and steel, responsibly sourced lithium and cobalt with third-party certification, and end-of-life battery recycling contracts with Redwood Materials or Li-Cycle. Full LCA disclosure to customers. (f) SCOR metrics: (i) Perfect order rate measuring complete, on-time, damage-free, correctly documented deliveries, targeting 95%+ at launch and 98%+ by year 3; (ii) Inventory days of supply for batteries and semiconductors, keeping 60-90 days for strategic items and 30 days for leverage items; (iii) Cash-to-cash cycle targeting negative through direct-to-customer prepayment model; (iv) Upside supply chain flexibility measuring days to respond to 20% unplanned demand change; (v) Scope 3 emissions per vehicle tracked quarterly for sustainability reporting. This integrated strategy mirrors the approach of successful EV startups (Rivian, Lucid) with adjustments based on lessons from their scaling struggles, particularly around supply chain maturity, chip supply, and first-year production ramp.
questions.rubric: Strategic fit with Fisher framework reference (3 points), sourcing strategy with Kraljic classification (4 points), manufacturing and distribution footprint (3 points), resilience and geopolitical strategy (3 points), sustainability positioning (3 points), three or more SCOR metrics with targets (4 points). Total 20 points. Credit for coherent integration of course concepts and realistic plan.

major_assignments.title: Assignment 1: Inventory Policy Design for a Multi-SKU Distribution Network
major_assignments.description: Design an inventory management policy for a regional distribution center serving 200 retail stores with 500 SKUs of consumer products. You will be given real demand data sets and must recommend EOQ, safety stock, and reorder point parameters for each SKU, classify SKUs using ABC analysis, and implement appropriate policies for different classes. The final deliverable is a complete inventory policy document with numerical recommendations, cost projections, and sensitivity analysis.
objectives: Apply EOQ, reorder point, and safety stock calculations to a realistic multi-SKU problem
Use ABC classification to differentiate inventory management effort across SKU importance
Compute total inventory cost and compare baseline to recommended policies
Demonstrate sensitivity analysis to key parameters (holding cost, service level, lead time)
Integrate risk pooling analysis into a centralization recommendation
requirements: Use the provided demand data file (500 SKUs x 52 weeks of history) to compute demand mean and std dev for each SKU
Classify SKUs into A, B, C based on annual dollar volume using Pareto analysis (A = top 80% of value, B = next 15%, C = last 5%)
Apply differentiated service levels: A = 99%, B = 95%, C = 90%
Compute EOQ, safety stock, reorder point, and average inventory investment for each SKU
Produce summary tables and charts showing inventory investment by class
Run sensitivity analysis varying the holding cost rate between 20% and 40% and report impact on total cost
Evaluate whether centralizing safety stock across the 200 stores (versus store-level) would reduce total inventory; quantify the savings
Submit as a PDF report of 10-15 pages plus an Excel workbook showing all calculations
deliverables: PDF report with executive summary, methodology, SKU-level analysis, recommendations, and appendices
Excel workbook with the full computational model, clearly documented
One-page executive summary suitable for a supply chain VP
Presentation slides (8-12 slides) summarizing key findings and recommendations

grading_rubric.description: Correct computation of demand statistics from the raw data; appropriate Pareto analysis and defensible class boundaries; clear visualization of the classification
Correct formulas applied with appropriate parameters; treatment of lead time variability; differentiated service levels by class; auditable Excel formulas
Total inventory cost comparison (baseline vs recommended) with clear reconciliation; separation of holding, ordering, and safety stock costs
Systematic variation of key parameters with tabulated results; identification of which parameters drive the recommendations
Correct application of the square root law; quantified savings estimate; discussion of trade-offs (transportation, service time)
Clear structure, professional visualizations, accessible executive summary, actionable recommendations for a supply chain leader audience

major_assignments.rubric: Total 100 points allocated across the six criteria above. Submissions late by 24 hours lose 10% penalty; late by 48 hours lose 25%; no submissions accepted after 72 hours without a documented emergency.

major_assignments.title: Assignment 2: Supply Chain Risk Assessment and Resilience Plan
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive supply chain risk assessment for a real publicly traded company of your choice (subject to instructor approval) and produce a resilience plan with prioritized recommendations. Your analysis must map the company's supply chain from tier-one through tier-three suppliers where public data allows, identify concentration risks and geopolitical exposures, and propose specific risk mitigation actions with cost estimates.
objectives: Conduct primary research on a real company's supply chain using public sources (10-K, supplier lists, news)
Identify and classify operational, disruption, financial, and geopolitical risks
Quantify concentration risk using appropriate metrics
Design specific resilience interventions and estimate their costs and benefits
Produce a prioritized action plan suitable for executive review
requirements: Select a publicly traded firm with non-trivial supply chain complexity (manufacturing, retail, tech); get instructor approval by week 9
Map the firm's major supply chain elements: key suppliers, manufacturing locations, distribution, customer base
Identify at least 15 specific risks across the four risk categories
Build a probability-impact matrix and prioritize the top 5 risks
Quantify at least two risks in dollar terms (expected loss, value at risk)
Propose mitigation actions for the top 5 risks with estimated costs and benefits
Evaluate the firm's disclosed risk management practices from its 10-K and sustainability reports
Submit a formal 15-20 page report plus an executive summary and presentation
deliverables: Risk assessment report with methodology, findings, and recommendations
Supply chain map showing key suppliers, facilities, and flows
Probability-impact matrix with top risks identified
Mitigation action plan with cost-benefit estimates
Executive summary and presentation for senior management audience

grading_rubric.criterion: Supply chain mapping and research quality
grading_rubric.description: Depth of research using public sources; identification of tier-one and deeper supplier relationships where possible; accuracy of facility and flow information
Breadth across all four risk categories; specificity of identified risks (not generic); evidence-based probability and impact assessments
Dollar quantification of at least two risks with transparent assumptions; use of appropriate metrics like concentration ratios, VaR, or expected loss
Specific actionable interventions tied to identified risks; cost estimates; benefit quantification; feasibility assessment
Report structure, visualizations, clarity of recommendations, quality of executive summary and slides

major_assignments.rubric: Total 100 points. Plagiarism check required for all research-based reports. Late submission penalties as in A1. Exceptional reports that identify non-obvious risks or provide novel quantification receive bonus consideration up to 5 extra points.

major_assignments.title: Assignment 3: Integrated Supply Chain Strategy Case Analysis
major_assignments.description: Comprehensive case analysis applying the full course toolkit to a contemporary supply chain strategy challenge. You will be given a detailed case study (released in week 12) and must develop an integrated recommendation covering strategy, network design, sourcing, operations, digital, sustainability, and risk. This is the capstone assignment integrating every topic in the course and serves as the final examination.
objectives: Integrate all course concepts into a coherent supply chain strategy
Apply quantitative tools (forecasting, inventory, network optimization) to a realistic scenario
Make defensible trade-offs across competing objectives (cost, service, resilience, sustainability)
Produce a complete strategic recommendation with financial and operational justification
Demonstrate mastery of the SCOR framework and supply chain KPIs
requirements: Analyze the assigned case (a real company facing a strategic supply chain decision)
Diagnose the current supply chain using the SCOR framework and identify performance gaps
Develop at least three alternative strategies and evaluate each on cost, service, resilience, and sustainability dimensions
Recommend one strategy with implementation roadmap covering 3-year horizon
Quantify financial impact including NPV, inventory investment, operating cost, and capital expenditure
Address all major course topics: strategy, forecasting, inventory, network, transportation, sourcing, contracts, bullwhip, risk, sustainability, digital, global
Submit a 20-25 page report plus appendices, executive summary, and presentation slides
Deliver a 15-minute presentation to a panel of faculty and industry practitioners in week 15
deliverables: Comprehensive written report (20-25 pages) covering all required topics
Full quantitative model (Excel or equivalent) supporting all financial claims

grading_rubric.description: Accurate assessment of current state using SCOR metrics; clear identification of gaps and root causes; use of course frameworks
Creativity and credibility of alternatives; rigorous comparison across cost, service, resilience, and sustainability; avoidance of false trade-offs
NPV and other financial metrics correctly computed; assumptions documented; sensitivity analysis on key parameters
Every major topic addressed substantively; concepts from multiple sessions connected into a coherent whole; not a checklist but an integrated strategy
Realistic sequence of actions over 3 years; identification of dependencies and risks; clear milestones and metrics
Professional writing, clear visualizations, effective executive summary, persuasive presentation delivery, handling of Q&A

major_assignments.rubric: Total 100 points. This capstone assignment is weighted heavily in the final course grade. Strong performance requires not just correct application of individual tools but genuine integration across the course. The panel will evaluate whether the student demonstrates the judgment of a supply chain leader, not just technical competence. Late submissions receive severe penalties since the presentation date is fixed.

course_policies.attendance_policy: Attendance is required at all scheduled class sessions. Case discussions and in-class exercises cannot be made up. Students may miss up to two classes without penalty; each additional absence beyond two reduces the participation grade by 3 percentage points. Documented emergencies, serious illness, and university-sponsored events are excused with advance notice and appropriate documentation.
course_policies.late_work_policy: Quizzes must be taken at the scheduled time unless a documented emergency prevents attendance. Assignments are due at 11:59 PM on the date specified in the syllabus. Late submissions are penalized 10% for the first 24 hours, 25% for the second 24 hours, and receive zero credit thereafter except in documented emergency situations approved by the instructor in advance.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be the student's own except where explicitly permitted as group work. Citations must be provided for all external sources, including textbooks, articles, websites, and AI assistants. Using generative AI tools for brainstorming and grammar check is permitted, but AI-generated content must be disclosed and cannot substitute for the student's own analytical work. Violations are referred to the university academic integrity office and may result in course failure.
course_policies.collaboration_policy: Study groups are encouraged for learning the material but all submitted work must be individual unless explicitly designated as a group assignment. For group assignments, all members must contribute substantively and a contribution statement is required. Sharing of solutions between individual assignments is a violation of academic integrity.
course_policies.accommodations_and_support: Students with documented disabilities should contact the Office of Student Disability Services at the start of the term to arrange accommodations. The instructor is committed to providing appropriate accommodations as determined by that office. Students struggling with the material are encouraged to attend office hours and use the course tutoring services; mental health and wellness resources are available through the university counseling center at no cost.
